British citizens of any race or [ethnicity](/wiki/Ethnicity "Ethnicity"). 2. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-i_anc_4-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-i_anc_4-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-i_anc_4-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-i_anc_4-3) [***e***](#cite_ref-i_anc_4-4) [***f***](#cite_ref-i_anc_4-5) People who identify of full or partial British ancestry born into that country. 3. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-i_born_7-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-i_born_7-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-i_born_7-2) UK-born people who identify of British ancestry only. 4. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-i_cit_11-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-i_cit_11-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-i_cit_11-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-i_cit_11-3) [***e***](#cite_ref-i_cit_11-4) [***f***](#cite_ref-i_cit_11-5) [***g***](#cite_ref-i_cit_11-6) British citizens or nationals. | |

or **Britons**, also known colloquially as **Brits**,[[22]](#cite_note-26) are the citizens and diaspora of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), the [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories"), and the [Crown dependencies](/wiki/Crown_dependencies "Crown dependencies").[[23]](#cite_note-27)[[24]](#cite_note-Dic62-28)[[25]](#cite_note-29) [British nationality law](/wiki/British_nationality_law "British nationality law") governs modern British citizenship and nationality, which can be acquired, for instance, by descent from British nationals. When used in a historical context, "British" or "Britons" can refer to the [Ancient Britons](/wiki/Ancient_Britons "Ancient Britons"), the [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages")-speaking inhabitants of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") during the [Iron Age](/wiki/British_Iron_Age "British Iron Age"), whose descendants formed the major part of the modern [Welsh people](/wiki/Welsh_people "Welsh people"), [Cornish people](/wiki/Cornish_people "Cornish people"), [Bretons](/wiki/Bretons "Bretons")[[24]](#cite_note-Dic62-28) and considerable proportions of [English people](/wiki/English_people "English people").[[26]](#cite_note-30)[[27]](#cite_note-31) It also refers to those [British subjects](/wiki/British_subjects "British subjects") born in parts of the former [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") that are now independent countries who settled in the United Kingdom prior to 1973.[[28]](#cite_note-windrushreport2020-32)

**British people** or **Britons**, also known colloquially as **Brits**,[[22]](#cite_note-26) are the citizens and diaspora of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), the [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories"), and the [Crown dependencies](/wiki/Crown_dependencies "Crown dependencies").[[23]](#cite_note-27)[[24]](#cite_note-Dic62-28)[[25]](#cite_note-29) [British nationality law](/wiki/British_nationality_law "British nationality law") governs modern British citizenship and nationality, which can be acquired, for instance, by descent from British nationals. When used in a historical context, "British" or "Britons" can refer to the [Ancient Britons](/wiki/Ancient_Britons "Ancient Britons"), the [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages")-speaking inhabitants of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") during the [Iron Age](/wiki/British_Iron_Age "British Iron Age"), whose descendants formed the major part of the modern [Welsh people](/wiki/Welsh_people "Welsh people"), [Cornish people](/wiki/Cornish_people "Cornish people"), [Bretons](/wiki/Bretons "Bretons")[[24]](#cite_note-Dic62-28) and considerable proportions of [English people](/wiki/English_people "English people").[[26]](#cite_note-30)[[27]](#cite_note-31) It also refers to those [British subjects](/wiki/British_subjects "British subjects") born in parts of the former [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") that are now independent countries who settled in the United Kingdom prior to 1973.[[28]](#cite_note-windrushreport2020-32)

Though early assertions of being British date from the [Late Middle Ages](/wiki/Late_Middle_Ages "Late Middle Ages"), the [Union of the Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns") in 1603 and the creation of the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") in 1707 triggered a sense of British national identity.[[29]](#cite_note-Colley1-33) The notion of [Britishness](/wiki/Britishness "Britishness") and a shared British identity was forged during the 18th century and early 19th century when Britain engaged in several global conflicts with France, and developed further during the [Victorian era](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era").[[29]](#cite_note-Colley1-33)[[30]](#cite_note-Colley5-34) The complex [history of the formation of the United Kingdom](/wiki/History_of_the_formation_of_the_United_Kingdom "History of the formation of the United Kingdom") created a "particular sense of nationhood and belonging" in Great Britain;[[29]](#cite_note-Colley1-33) Britishness became "superimposed on much older identities", of [English](/wiki/English_people "English people"), [Scots](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") and [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_people "Welsh people") cultures, whose distinctiveness still resists notions of a [homogenised](/wiki/Homogeneity_(ecology) "Homogeneity (ecology)") British identity.[[31]](#cite_note-Colley6-35) Because of longstanding ethno-sectarian divisions, British identity in [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") is controversial, but it is held with strong conviction by [Unionists](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland").[[32]](#cite_note-36)

Modern Britons are descended mainly from the varied ethnic groups that settled in [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") in and before the 11th century: [Prehistoric](/wiki/Prehistoric_Britain "Prehistoric Britain"), Brittonic, [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain"), [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons"), [Norse](/wiki/Norsemen "Norsemen"), and [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans").[[33]](#cite_note-state-37) The progressive political unification of the British Isles facilitated migration, cultural and linguistic exchange, and intermarriage between the peoples of England, Scotland and Wales during the late Middle Ages, [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_Britain "Early modern Britain") and beyond.[[34]](#cite_note-Trudgill519-38)[[35]](#cite_note-Richardson531-39) Since 1922 and earlier, there has been [immigration to the United Kingdom](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_Kingdom_since_1922 "Immigration to the United Kingdom since 1922") by people from what is now the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland"), the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"), mainland Europe and elsewhere; they and their descendants are mostly British citizens, with some assuming a British, dual or hyphenated identity.[[36]](#cite_note-Ward113-115-40) This includes the groups [Black British](/wiki/Black_British "Black British") and [Asian British people](/wiki/Asian-British_people "Asian-British people"), which together constitute around 10% of the British population.[[37]](#cite_note-41)

The British are a diverse, [multinational](/wiki/Multinational_state "Multinational state"),[[38]](#cite_note-42)[[39]](#cite_note-43) multicultural and multilingual people, with "strong regional accents, expressions and identities".[[40]](#cite_note-44)[[41]](#cite_note-Rosen3-45) The [social structure of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Social_structure_of_the_United_Kingdom "Social structure of the United Kingdom") has changed radically since the 19th century, with a decline in religious observance, enlargement of the [middle class](/wiki/Middle_class "Middle class"), and [increased ethnic diversity](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_the_United_Kingdom "Ethnic groups in the United Kingdom"), particularly since the 1950s, when citizens of the British Empire were encouraged to immigrate to Britain to work as part of the recovery from World War II. The population of the UK stands at around 67 million,[[42]](#cite_note-46) with around 50 million being [White British](/wiki/White_British "White British"). This includes 44.4 million in England and Wales as of [2021](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_census "2021 United Kingdom census"),[[43]](#cite_note-47) and 4.2 million in Scotland as of 2022.[[44]](#cite_note-48) 1.8 million identify as White in Northern Ireland, including White British and other White ethnicities, as of 2021.[[45]](#cite_note-49) Outside of the UK, the [British diaspora](/wiki/British_diaspora "British diaspora") totals around 200 million with higher concentrations in the United States, Australia, Canada, and New Zealand, with smaller concentrations in the Republic of Ireland, Chile, South Africa, and parts of the Caribbean.[[1]](#cite_note-Richards-1)

## History of the term

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History of the term")]

Further information: [Britain (place name)](/wiki/Britain_(place_name) "Britain (place name)")

See also: [Glossary of names for the British](/wiki/Glossary_of_names_for_the_British "Glossary of names for the British")

The earliest known reference to the inhabitants of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") may have come from 4th century BC records of the voyage of [Pytheas](/wiki/Pytheas "Pytheas"), a [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") geographer who made a voyage of exploration around the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"). Although none of his own writings remain, writers during the time of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") made much reference to them. Pytheas called the islands collectively αἱ Βρεττανίαι (*hai Brettaniai*), which has been translated as the *Brittanic Isles*, and the peoples of what are today England, [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") and the [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man") of *Prettanike* were called the Πρεττανοί (*Prettanoi*), *Priteni*, *Pritani* or *Pretani*.

The group included [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), which was referred to as *Ierne* (*Insula sacra* "sacred island" as the Greeks interpreted it) "inhabited by the different race of *Hiberni*" (*gens hibernorum*), and Britain as *insula Albionum*, "island of the Albions".[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnyder2003-50)[[47]](#cite_note-ohi-51) The term *Pritani* may have reached Pytheas from the [Gauls](/wiki/Gaul "Gaul"), who possibly used it as their term for the inhabitants of the islands.[[47]](#cite_note-ohi-51)

[Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") and [Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") writers, in the 1st century BC and the 1st century AD, name the inhabitants of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") and [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland") as the *[Priteni](/wiki/Cruthin "Cruthin")*,[[48]](#cite_note-52) the origin of the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") word *Britanni*. It has been suggested that this name derives from a [Gaulish](/wiki/Gaulish_language "Gaulish language") description translated as "people of the forms", referring to the custom of tattooing or painting their bodies with blue woad made from *[Isatis tinctoria](/wiki/Isatis_tinctoria "Isatis tinctoria")*.[[49]](#cite_note-53) [Parthenius](/wiki/Parthenius_of_Nicaea "Parthenius of Nicaea"), an Ancient Greek grammarian, and the *[Etymologicum Genuinum](/wiki/Etymologicum_Genuinum "Etymologicum Genuinum")*, a 9th-century lexical encyclopaedia, mention a mythical character Bretannus (the Latinised form of the [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Βρεττανός, *Brettanós*) as the father of [Celtine](/wiki/Celtine "Celtine"), mother of Celtus, the eponymous ancestor of the [Celts](/wiki/Celts "Celts").[[50]](#cite_note-54)

By 50 BC, Greek geographers were using equivalents of *Prettanikē* as a collective name for the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles").[[51]](#cite_note-55) However, with the [Roman conquest of Britain](/wiki/Roman_conquest_of_Britain "Roman conquest of Britain"), the Latin term *[Britannia](/wiki/Britannia "Britannia")* was used for the island of Great Britain, and later Roman-occupied Britain south of [Caledonia](/wiki/Caledonia "Caledonia") (modern day Scotland north of the rivers Forth and Clyde), although the people of Caledonia and the north were also the selfsame Britons during the Roman period, the Gaels not arriving until four centuries later.[[52]](#cite_note-56)[[53]](#cite_note-57) Following the [end of Roman rule in Britain](/wiki/End_of_Roman_rule_in_Britain "End of Roman rule in Britain"), the island of Great Britain was left open to invasion by [pagan](/wiki/Paganism "Paganism"), seafaring warriors such as [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages")-speaking [Anglo-Saxons](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons") and [Jutes](/wiki/Jutes "Jutes") from [Continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe"), who gained control in areas around the south east, and to [Middle Irish](/wiki/Middle_Irish "Middle Irish")-speaking people migrating from the north of Ireland to the north of Great Britain, founding Gaelic kingdoms such as [Dál Riata](/wiki/D%C3%A1l_Riata "Dál Riata") and [Alba](/wiki/Alba "Alba"), which would eventually subsume the native Brittonic and Pictish kingdoms and become Scotland.[[54]](#cite_note-sax1-58)

In this [sub-Roman Britain](/wiki/Sub-Roman_Britain "Sub-Roman Britain"), as Anglo-Saxon culture spread across southern and eastern Britain and Gaelic through much of the north, the demonym "Briton" became restricted to the Brittonic-speaking inhabitants of what would later be called [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall"), [North West England](/wiki/North_West_England "North West England") ([Cumbria](/wiki/Cumbria "Cumbria")), and a southern part of [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland")[[55]](#cite_note-59) ([Strathclyde](/wiki/Strathclyde "Strathclyde")).[[56]](#cite_note-SnowCelt-60) In addition, the term was also applied to [Brittany](/wiki/Brittany "Brittany") in what is today [France](/wiki/France "France") and [Britonia](/wiki/Britonia "Britonia") in north west [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), both regions having been colonised in the 5th century by Britons fleeing the Anglo-Saxon invasions. However, the term "Britannia" persisted as the Latin name for the island. The *[Historia Brittonum](/wiki/Historia_Brittonum "Historia Brittonum")* claimed legendary origins as a prestigious [genealogy](/wiki/Genealogy "Genealogy") for [Brittonic kings](/wiki/King_of_the_Britons "King of the Britons"), followed by the *[Historia Regum Britanniae](/wiki/Historia_Regum_Britanniae "Historia Regum Britanniae")*, which popularised this pseudo-history to support the claims of the [Kings of England](/wiki/List_of_English_monarchs "List of English monarchs").[[57]](#cite_note-Consci1-61)

During the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), and particularly in the [Tudor period](/wiki/Tudor_period "Tudor period"), the term "British" was used to refer to the [Welsh people](/wiki/Welsh_people "Welsh people") and [Cornish people](/wiki/Cornish_people "Cornish people"). At that time, it was "the long held belief that these were the remaining descendants of the Britons and that they spoke '[the British tongue](/wiki/Common_Brittonic "Common Brittonic")'".[[57]](#cite_note-Consci1-61) This notion was supported by texts such as the *[Historia Regum Britanniae](/wiki/Historia_Regum_Britanniae "Historia Regum Britanniae")*, a [pseudohistorical](/wiki/Pseudohistory "Pseudohistory") account of ancient British history, written in the mid-12th century by [Geoffrey of Monmouth](/wiki/Geoffrey_of_Monmouth "Geoffrey of Monmouth").[[57]](#cite_note-Consci1-61) The *Historia Regum Britanniae* chronicled the lives of [legendary kings of the Britons](/wiki/List_of_legendary_kings_of_Britain "List of legendary kings of Britain") in a narrative spanning 2000 years, beginning with the [Trojans](/wiki/Troy "Troy") founding the ancient British nation and continuing until the [Anglo-Saxon settlement of Britain](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_settlement_of_Britain "Anglo-Saxon settlement of Britain") in the 7th century forced the Britons to the west, i.e. [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") and [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall"), and north, i.e. [Cumbria](/wiki/Cumbria "Cumbria"), [Strathclyde](/wiki/Strathclyde "Strathclyde") and northern Scotland.[[57]](#cite_note-Consci1-61) This legendary Celtic history of Great Britain is known as the [Matter of Britain](/wiki/Matter_of_Britain "Matter of Britain"). The Matter of Britain, a [national myth](/wiki/National_myth "National myth"), was retold or reinterpreted in works by [Gerald of Wales](/wiki/Gerald_of_Wales "Gerald of Wales"), a [Cambro-Norman](/wiki/Cambro-Normans "Cambro-Normans") chronicler who, in the 12th and 13th centuries, used the term "British" to refer to the people later known as the Welsh.[[58]](#cite_note-62)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Ancestral roots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ancestral roots")]

Further information: [Genetic history of the British Isles](/wiki/Genetic_history_of_the_British_Isles "Genetic history of the British Isles") and [historical immigration to Great Britain](/wiki/Historical_immigration_to_Great_Britain "Historical immigration to Great Britain")

The indigenous people of the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles") have a combination of [Celtic](/wiki/Celts "Celts"), [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon "Anglo-Saxon"), [Norse](/wiki/Vikings "Vikings") and [Norman](/wiki/Normans "Normans") ancestry.[[56]](#cite_note-SnowCelt-60)[[59]](#cite_note-Parliament_1-63)[[60]](#cite_note-Mebyon_Kernow_1-64)[[61]](#cite_note-RTÉ_1-65)[[62]](#cite_note-Welsh_Government_1-66)[[63]](#cite_note-United-67)[[64]](#cite_note-Oppenheimer-68) Between the 8th and 11th centuries, "three major cultural divisions" emerged in Great Britain: the [English](/wiki/English_people "English people"), the [Scots](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") and the [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_people "Welsh people"). The earlier Brittonic Celtic polities in what are today England and Scotland were absorbed into Anglo-Saxon England and Gaelic Scotland by the early 11th century.[[65]](#cite_note-Smyth24-25-69) The English had been unified under a single [nation state](/wiki/Nation_state "Nation state") in 937 by King [Athelstan of Wessex](/wiki/Athelstan_of_England "Athelstan of England") after the [Battle of Brunanburh](/wiki/Battle_of_Brunanburh "Battle of Brunanburh").[[66]](#cite_note-70)

Before then, the English (known then in [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") as the *Anglecynn*) were under the governance of independent Anglo-Saxon [petty kingdoms](/wiki/Petty_kingdom "Petty kingdom"), which gradually coalesced into a [Heptarchy](/wiki/Heptarchy "Heptarchy") of seven powerful states, the most powerful of which were [Mercia](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia") and [Wessex](/wiki/Wessex "Wessex"). Scottish historian [Neil Oliver](/wiki/Neil_Oliver "Neil Oliver") said that the Battle of Brunanburh would "define the shape of Britain into the modern era"; it was a "showdown for two very different ethnic identities – a Norse Celtic alliance versus Anglo Saxon. It aimed to settle once and for all whether Britain would be controlled by a single imperial power or remain several separate independent kingdoms, a split in perceptions which is still very much with us today".[[67]](#cite_note-71) However, historian [Simon Schama](/wiki/Simon_Schama "Simon Schama") suggested that it was [Edward I of England](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England") who solely was "responsible for provoking the peoples of Britain into an awareness of their nationhood" in the 13th century.[[68]](#cite_note-SchamaNations-72) Schama hypothesised that [Scottish national identity](/wiki/Scottish_national_identity "Scottish national identity"), "a complex amalgam" of [Gaelic](/wiki/Gaelic_Ireland "Gaelic Ireland"), [Brittonic](/wiki/Britons_(Celtic_people) "Britons (Celtic people)"), [Pictish](/wiki/Pictish "Pictish"), [Norsemen](/wiki/Norsemen "Norsemen") and [Anglo-Norman](/wiki/Anglo-Normans "Anglo-Normans") origins, was not finally forged until the [Wars of Scottish Independence](/wiki/Wars_of_Scottish_Independence "Wars of Scottish Independence") against the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") in the late 13th and early 14th centuries.[[69]](#cite_note-73)[[70]](#cite_note-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Arth_tapestry2.jpg/250px-Arth_tapestry2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arth_tapestry2.jpg)

Medieval tapestry showing [King Arthur](/wiki/King_Arthur "King Arthur"), a legendary [ancient British](/wiki/Britons_(historical) "Britons (historical)") ruler who had a leading role in the [Matter of Britain](/wiki/Matter_of_Britain "Matter of Britain"), a [national myth](/wiki/National_myth "National myth") used as propaganda for the ancestral origins of the [British royal family](/wiki/British_royal_family "British royal family") and their [British subjects](/wiki/British_subject "British subject")

Though [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") was conquered by England, and its legal system replaced by that of the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") under the [Laws in Wales Acts 1535–1542](/wiki/Laws_in_Wales_Acts_1535%E2%80%931542 "Laws in Wales Acts 1535–1542"), the Welsh endured as a [nation](/wiki/Nation "Nation") distinct from [the English](/wiki/The_English "The English"); and to some degree the [Cornish people](/wiki/Cornish_people "Cornish people"), although conquered into England by the 11th century, also retained a distinct Brittonic identity and language.[[71]](#cite_note-Consci11-75) Later, with both an [English Reformation](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation") and a [Scottish Reformation](/wiki/Scottish_Reformation "Scottish Reformation"), [Edward VI of England](/wiki/Edward_VI_of_England "Edward VI of England"), under the counsel of [Edward Seymour, 1st Duke of Somerset](/wiki/Edward_Seymour,_1st_Duke_of_Somerset "Edward Seymour, 1st Duke of Somerset"), advocated a union with the [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland"), joining England, Wales and Scotland in a united Protestant Great Britain.[[72]](#cite_note-Ryrie82-76) The Duke of Somerset supported the unification of the English, Welsh and Scots under the "indifferent old name of Britons" on the basis that their monarchies "both derived from a [Pre-Roman](/wiki/Roman_empire "Roman empire") British monarchy".[[72]](#cite_note-Ryrie82-76)

Following the death of [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I") of England in 1603, the throne of England was inherited by James VI, King of Scots, so that the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") and the [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") were united in a [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") under [James VI of Scotland and I of England](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I"), an event referred to as the [Union of the Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns").[[73]](#cite_note-77) King James advocated full [political union](/wiki/Political_union "Political union") between England and Scotland,[[74]](#cite_note-78) and on 20 October 1604 proclaimed his assumption of the [style](/wiki/Style_(manner_of_address) "Style (manner of address)") "King of Great Britain", though this title was rejected by both the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") and the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland")[[75]](#cite_note-79)[[76]](#cite_note-80) and thus had no basis in either [English law](/wiki/English_law "English law") or [Scots law](/wiki/Scots_law "Scots law").

### Union and the development of Britishness

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Union and the development of Britishness")]

Main articles: [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union") and [Britishness](/wiki/Britishness "Britishness")

See also: [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") and [History of the formation of the United Kingdom](/wiki/History_of_the_formation_of_the_United_Kingdom "History of the formation of the United Kingdom")

Further information: [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy"), and [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Flag_of_Great_Britain_%281707%E2%80%931800%29.svg/250px-Flag_of_Great_Britain_%281707%E2%80%931800%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Great_Britain_(1707%E2%80%931800).svg)

On 12 April 1606, the [Union Flag](/wiki/Union_Flag "Union Flag") representing the [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") between the Kingdoms of [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") and [Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") was specified in a royal decree. The [St George's Cross](/wiki/St_George%27s_Cross "St George's Cross") and [St Andrew's saltire](/wiki/Flag_of_Scotland "Flag of Scotland") were "joined together ... to be published to our Subjects."[[77]](#cite_note-flaginst-81)

Despite centuries of military and religious conflict, the Kingdoms of England and Scotland had been "drawing increasingly together" since the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") of the 16th century and the Union of the Crowns in 1603.[[78]](#cite_note-Colley12-82) A broadly shared language, island, monarch, religion and Bible (the [Authorized King James Version](/wiki/Authorized_King_James_Version "Authorized King James Version")) further contributed to a growing cultural alliance between the two sovereign realms and their peoples.[[78]](#cite_note-Colley12-82)[[79]](#cite_note-Parli-83) The [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") of 1688 resulted in a pair of Acts of the English and Scottish legislatures—the [Bill of Rights 1689](/wiki/Bill_of_Rights_1689 "Bill of Rights 1689") and [Claim of Right Act 1689](/wiki/Claim_of_Right_Act_1689 "Claim of Right Act 1689") respectively—that ensured that the shared [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") of England and Scotland was held only by Protestants. Despite this, although popular with the monarchy and much of the aristocracy, attempts to unite the two states by Acts of Parliament were unsuccessful in 1606, 1667 and 1689;[[79]](#cite_note-Parli-83) increased political management of Scottish affairs from England had led to "criticism" and had strained Anglo-Scottish relations.[[80]](#cite_note-Scot18th-84)[[81]](#cite_note-85)

While English maritime explorations during the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery") gave new-found imperial power and wealth to the English and Welsh at the end of the 17th century, Scotland suffered from a long-standing weak economy.[[80]](#cite_note-Scot18th-84) In response, the Scottish kingdom, in opposition to [William II of Scotland (III of England)](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England"), commenced the [Darien Scheme](/wiki/Darien_Scheme "Darien Scheme"), an attempt to establish a Scottish imperial outlet—the [colony](/wiki/Colony "Colony") of New Caledonia—on the [isthmus of Panama](/wiki/Isthmus_of_Panama "Isthmus of Panama").[[80]](#cite_note-Scot18th-84) However, through a combination of disease, Spanish hostility, Scottish mismanagement and opposition to the scheme by the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") and the English government (who did not want to provoke the Spanish into war)[[80]](#cite_note-Scot18th-84)[[82]](#cite_note-Birnie-86) this imperial venture ended in "catastrophic failure", with an estimated "25% of Scotland's total liquid capital" lost.[[80]](#cite_note-Scot18th-84)

The events of the Darien Scheme, and the passing by the English Parliament of the [Act of Settlement 1701](/wiki/Act_of_Settlement_1701 "Act of Settlement 1701") asserting the right to choose the [order of succession](/wiki/Order_of_succession "Order of succession") for English, Scottish and Irish thrones, escalated political hostilities between England and Scotland and neutralised calls for a united British people. The Parliament of Scotland responded by passing the [Act of Security 1704](/wiki/Act_of_Security_1704 "Act of Security 1704"), allowing it to appoint a different monarch to succeed to the Scottish crown from that of England if it so wished.[[80]](#cite_note-Scot18th-84) The English political perspective was that the appointment of a [Jacobite](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism") monarchy in Scotland opened up the possibility of a Franco-Scottish military conquest of England during the [Second Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Second_Hundred_Years%27_War "Second Hundred Years' War") and [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession").[[80]](#cite_note-Scot18th-84) The Parliament of England passed the [Alien Act 1705](/wiki/Alien_Act_1705 "Alien Act 1705"), which provided that Scottish nationals in England were to be treated as [aliens](/wiki/Alien_(law) "Alien (law)") and estates held by Scots would be treated as alien property,[[83]](#cite_note-87) whilst also restricting the [import](/wiki/Import "Import") of Scottish products into England and its colonies (about half of Scotland's trade).[[84]](#cite_note-88) However, the Act contained a provision that it would be suspended if the Parliament of Scotland entered into negotiations regarding the creation of a unified [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain"), which in turn would refund Scottish financial losses on the Darien Scheme.[[82]](#cite_note-Birnie-86)

### Union of Scotland and England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Union of Scotland and England")]

Despite opposition from within both Scotland[[80]](#cite_note-Scot18th-84) and England,[[85]](#cite_note-Colley1213-89) a [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union") was agreed in 1706 and was then ratified by the parliaments of both countries with the passing of the [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707"). With effect from 1 May 1707, this created a new sovereign state called the "[Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain")".[[86]](#cite_note-90)[[87]](#cite_note-91)[[88]](#cite_note-92) This kingdom "began as a hostile merger", but led to a "full partnership in the most powerful [going concern](/wiki/Going_concern "Going concern") in the world"; historian [Simon Schama](/wiki/Simon_Schama "Simon Schama") stated that "it was one of the most astonishing transformations in European history".[[89]](#cite_note-Schama-93) After 1707, a British national identity began to develop, though it was initially resisted, particularly by the English.[[85]](#cite_note-Colley1213-89) The peoples of Great Britain had by the 1750s begun to assume a "layered identity": to think of themselves as simultaneously British and also Scottish, English, or Welsh.[[85]](#cite_note-Colley1213-89)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Turner%2C_The_Battle_of_Trafalgar_%281822%29.jpg/250px-Turner%2C_The_Battle_of_Trafalgar_%281822%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Turner,_The_Battle_of_Trafalgar_(1822).jpg)

*[The Battle of Trafalgar](/wiki/The_Battle_of_Trafalgar_(Turner) "The Battle of Trafalgar (Turner)")* by [J. M. W. Turner](/wiki/J._M._W._Turner "J. M. W. Turner") (oil on canvas, 1822–1824) combines events from several moments during the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars")' [Battle of Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar")—a major British naval victory upon which Britishness has drawn influence.

The terms [North Briton](/wiki/North_Briton "North Briton") and [South Briton](/wiki/South_Briton "South Briton") were devised for the Scots and the English respectively, with the former gaining some preference in Scotland, particularly by the economists and philosophers of the [Scottish Enlightenment](/wiki/Scottish_Enlightenment "Scottish Enlightenment").[[90]](#cite_note-94)[[91]](#cite_note-95) Indeed, it was the "Scots [who] played key roles in shaping the contours of British identity";[[92]](#cite_note-Gottlieb15-96) "their scepticism about the Union allowed the Scots the space and time in which
to dominate the construction of Britishness in its early crucial years",[[93]](#cite_note-97) drawing upon the notion of a shared "spirit of liberty common to both Saxon and Celt ... against the usurpation of the Church of Rome".[[94]](#cite_note-Brad1-98) [James Thomson](/wiki/James_Thomson_(poet,_born_1700) "James Thomson (poet, born 1700)") was a poet and playwright born to a [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland") minister in the [Scottish Lowlands](/wiki/Scottish_Lowlands "Scottish Lowlands") in 1700 who was interested in forging a common British culture and national identity in this way.[[94]](#cite_note-Brad1-98) In collaboration with [Thomas Arne](/wiki/Thomas_Arne "Thomas Arne"), they wrote *[Alfred](/wiki/Alfred_(Arne_opera) "Alfred (Arne opera)")*, an opera about [Alfred the Great](/wiki/Alfred_the_Great "Alfred the Great")'s victory against the [Vikings](/wiki/Viking "Viking") performed to [Frederick, Prince of Wales](/wiki/Frederick,_Prince_of_Wales "Frederick, Prince of Wales") in 1740 to commemorate the accession of [George I](/wiki/George_I_of_Great_Britain "George I of Great Britain") and the birthday of [Princess Augusta](/wiki/Princess_Augusta_of_Great_Britain "Princess Augusta of Great Britain").[[95]](#cite_note-99) "[Rule, Britannia!](/wiki/Rule,_Britannia! "Rule, Britannia!")" was the climactic piece of the opera and quickly became a "[jingoistic](/wiki/Jingoism "Jingoism")" British patriotic song celebrating "Britain's supremacy offshore".[[96]](#cite_note-Colley10-100) An [island country](/wiki/Island_country "Island country") with a series of victories for the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") associated empire and [naval warfare](/wiki/Naval_warfare "Naval warfare") "inextricably with ideals of Britishness and Britain's place in the world".[[97]](#cite_note-101)[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEColley1992132–133-102)

[Britannia](/wiki/Britannia "Britannia"), the new [national personification](/wiki/National_personification "National personification") of Great Britain, was established in the 1750s as a representation of "nation and empire rather than any single national hero".[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhaleCopley199281-103) On Britannia and British identity, historian Peter Borsay wrote:

> Up until 1797 Britannia was conventionally depicted holding a spear, but as a consequence of the increasingly prominent role of the Royal Navy in the war against the French, and of several spectacular victories, the spear was replaced by a trident... The navy had come to be seen...as the very bulwark of British liberty and the essence of what it was to be British.[[100]](#cite_note-104)

From the Union of 1707 through to the [Battle of Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo") in 1815, Great Britain was "involved in successive, very dangerous wars with Catholic France",[[101]](#cite_note-Colley18-105) but which "all brought enough military and naval victories ... to flatter British pride".[[102]](#cite_note-Colley52-106) As the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") with the [First French Empire](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire") advanced, "the English and Scottish learned to define themselves as similar primarily by virtue of not being French or Catholic".[[103]](#cite_note-Allan17-107) In combination with sea power and empire, the notion of Britishness became more "closely bound up with Protestantism",[[104]](#cite_note-Colley8-108) a cultural commonality through which the English, Scots and Welsh became "fused together, and remain[ed] so, despite their many cultural divergences".[[105]](#cite_note-Colley386-109)

The neo-classical monuments that proliferated at the end of the 18th century and the start of the 19th century, such as [The Kymin](/wiki/The_Kymin "The Kymin") at [Monmouth](/wiki/Monmouth "Monmouth"), were attempts to meld the concepts of Britishness with the [Greco-Roman empires](/wiki/Greco-Roman_world "Greco-Roman world") of [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity"). The new and expanding [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") provided "unprecedented opportunities for upward mobility and the accumulations of wealth", and so the "Scottish, Welsh and Irish populations were prepared to suppress nationalist issues on pragmatic grounds".[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERojek20088-110) The British Empire was "crucial to the idea of a British identity and to the self-image of Britishness".[[107]](#cite_note-111) Indeed, the Scottish welcomed Britishness during the 19th century "for it offered a context within which they could hold on to their own identity whilst participating in, and benefiting from, the expansion of the [British] Empire".[[108]](#cite_note-Williams17-112) Similarly, the "new emphasis of Britishness was broadly welcomed by the Welsh who considered themselves to be the lineal descendants of the ancient Britons – a word that was still used to refer exclusively to the Welsh".[[108]](#cite_note-Williams17-112) For the English, however, by the [Victorian era](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era") their enthusiastic adoption of Britishness had meant that, for them, Britishness "meant the same as 'Englishness'",[[109]](#cite_note-113)[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaunce_et_al._200492-114) so much so that "Englishness and Britishness" and "'England' and 'Britain' were used interchangeably in a variety of contexts".[[111]](#cite_note-Anderson34-115) England has "always been the dominant component of the British Isles in terms of size, population and power"; [Magna Carta](/wiki/Magna_Carta "Magna Carta"), [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") and hostility to [continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe") were English factors that influenced British sensibilities.[[112]](#cite_note-Langlands-116)[[113]](#cite_note-Ichijo26-117)

### Union with Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Union with Ireland")]

The [political union in 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") of the predominantly Catholic [Kingdom of Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") with Great Britain, coupled with the outbreak of peace with France in the early 19th century, challenged the previous century's concept of militant Protestant Britishness.[[114]](#cite_note-Colley322-118)[[115]](#cite_note-Spohn22-119) The new, expanded [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") meant that the state had to re-evaluate its position on the civil rights of Catholics, and extend its definition of Britishness to the [Irish people](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people").[[115]](#cite_note-Spohn22-119)[[116]](#cite_note-Howe40-120) Like the terms that had been invented at the time of the Acts of Union 1707, "[West Briton](/wiki/West_Brit "West Brit")" was introduced for the Irish after 1800. In 1832 [Daniel O'Connell](/wiki/Daniel_O%27Connell "Daniel O'Connell"), an Irish politician who campaigned for [Catholic Emancipation](/wiki/Catholic_Emancipation "Catholic Emancipation"), stated in Britain's [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"):

> [The people of Ireland](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people") are ready to become a portion of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), provided they be made so in reality and not in name alone; they are ready to become a kind of West Briton if made so in benefits and justice; but if not, we are Irishmen again.[[117]](#cite_note-121)

[Ireland, from 1801 to 1923](/wiki/History_of_Ireland_(1801%E2%80%931923) "History of Ireland (1801–1923)"), was marked by a succession of economic and political mismanagement and neglect, which marginalised the Irish,[[116]](#cite_note-Howe40-120) and advanced [Irish nationalism](/wiki/Irish_nationalism "Irish nationalism"). In the forty years that followed the Union, successive British governments grappled with the problems of governing a country which had as [Benjamin Disraeli](/wiki/Benjamin_Disraeli "Benjamin Disraeli"), a staunch anti-Irish and anti-Catholic member of the Conservative party with a virulent racial and religious prejudice towards Ireland[[118]](#cite_note-Blake2012-122) put it in 1844, "a starving population, an absentee aristocracy, and an alien Church, and in addition the weakest executive in the world".[[119]](#cite_note-123) Although the vast majority of [Unionists in Ireland](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland") proclaimed themselves "simultaneously Irish and British", even for them there was a strain upon the adoption of Britishness after the [Great Famine](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)").[[120]](#cite_note-124)

War continued to be a unifying factor for the people of Great Britain: British jingoism re-emerged during the [Boer Wars](/wiki/Boer_Wars "Boer Wars") in [southern Africa](/wiki/Southern_Africa "Southern Africa").[[121]](#cite_note-Ward96-125)[[122]](#cite_note-Ward16-126) The experience of military, political and economic power from the rise of the British Empire led to a very specific drive in artistic technique, taste and sensibility for Britishness.[[123]](#cite_note-taste-127) In 1887, [Frederic Harrison](/wiki/Frederic_Harrison "Frederic Harrison") wrote:

> Morally, we Britons plant the British flag on every peak and pass; and wherever the Union Jack floats there we place the cardinal British institutions—tea, tubs, sanitary appliances, lawn tennis, and churches.[[111]](#cite_note-Anderson34-115)

The [Roman Catholic Relief Act 1829](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Relief_Act_1829 "Roman Catholic Relief Act 1829") reflected a "marked change in attitudes" in Great Britain towards Catholics and Catholicism.[[124]](#cite_note-Colley324-128) A "significant" example of this was the collaboration between [Augustus Welby Pugin](/wiki/Augustus_Welby_Northmore_Pugin "Augustus Welby Northmore Pugin"), an "ardent Roman Catholic" and son of a Frenchman, and Sir [Charles Barry](/wiki/Charles_Barry "Charles Barry"), "a confirmed Protestant", in redesigning the [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster")—"the building that most enshrines ... Britain's national and imperial pre-tensions".[[124]](#cite_note-Colley324-128) Protestantism gave way to [imperialism](/wiki/Imperialism "Imperialism") as the leading element of British national identity during the Victorian and [Edwardian eras](/wiki/Edwardian_era "Edwardian era"),[[122]](#cite_note-Ward16-126) and as such, a series of royal, imperial and national celebrations were introduced to the British people to assert imperial British culture and give themselves a sense of uniqueness, superiority and national consciousness.[[115]](#cite_note-Spohn22-119)[[122]](#cite_note-Ward16-126)[[125]](#cite_note-129) [Empire Day](/wiki/Empire_Day "Empire Day") and jubilees of [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") were introduced to the British [middle class](/wiki/Middle_class "Middle class"),[[122]](#cite_note-Ward16-126) but quickly "merged into a national 'tradition'".[[126]](#cite_note-130)

### Modern period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Modern period")]

See also: [British nationality law](/wiki/British_nationality_law "British nationality law")

Further information: [Immigration to the United Kingdom since 1922](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_Kingdom_since_1922 "Immigration to the United Kingdom since 1922")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/30a_Sammlung_Eybl_Gro%C3%9Fbritannien._Alfred_Leete_%281882%E2%80%931933%29_Britons_%28Kitchener%29_wants_you_%28Briten_Kitchener_braucht_Euch%29._1914_%28Nachdruck%29%2C_74_x_50_cm._%28Slg.Nr._552%29.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:30a_Sammlung_Eybl_Gro%C3%9Fbritannien._Alfred_Leete_(1882%E2%80%931933)_Britons_(Kitchener)_wants_you_(Briten_Kitchener_braucht_Euch)._1914_(Nachdruck),_74_x_50_cm._(Slg.Nr._552).jpg)

A famous [First World War](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I")-era [recruitment poster](/wiki/Lord_Kitchener_Wants_You "Lord Kitchener Wants You"), stressing the concept of British national identity

The [First World War](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") "reinforced the sense of Britishness" and patriotism in the early 20th century.[[115]](#cite_note-Spohn22-119)[[121]](#cite_note-Ward96-125) Through war service (including conscription in Great Britain), "the English, Welsh, Scots and Irish fought as British".[[115]](#cite_note-Spohn22-119) The aftermath of the war institutionalised British national commemoration through [Remembrance Sunday](/wiki/Remembrance_Sunday "Remembrance Sunday") and the [Poppy Appeal](/wiki/Poppy_Appeal "Poppy Appeal").[[115]](#cite_note-Spohn22-119) The [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") had a similar unifying effect upon the British people,[[127]](#cite_note-Spohn23-131) however, its outcome was to recondition Britishness on a basis of [democratic values](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") and its marked contrast to [Europeanism](/wiki/Europeanism "Europeanism").[[127]](#cite_note-Spohn23-131) Notions that the British "constituted an Island race, and that it stood for democracy were reinforced during the war and they were circulated in the country through [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill")'s speeches, history books and newspapers".[[127]](#cite_note-Spohn23-131)

At its international zenith, "Britishness joined peoples around the world in shared traditions and common loyalties that were strenuously maintained".[[128]](#cite_note-132) But following the two world wars, the British Empire experienced rapid [decolonisation](/wiki/Decolonisation "Decolonisation"). The secession of the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") from the United Kingdom meant that Britishness had lost "its Irish dimension" in 1922,[[127]](#cite_note-Spohn23-131) and the shrinking empire supplanted by independence movements dwindled the appeal of British identity in the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") during the mid-20th century.[[129]](#cite_note-133)

Since the [British Nationality Act 1948](/wiki/British_Nationality_Act_1948 "British Nationality Act 1948") and the subsequent mass [immigration to the United Kingdom](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_Kingdom_since_1922 "Immigration to the United Kingdom since 1922") from the Commonwealth and elsewhere in the world, "the expression and experience of cultural life in Britain has become fragmented and reshaped by the influences of gender, ethnicity, class and region".[[130]](#cite_note-134) Furthermore, the United Kingdom's membership of the [European Economic Community](/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community") in 1973 eroded the concept of Britishness as distinct from [continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe").[[131]](#cite_note-135)[[132]](#cite_note-136) As such, since the 1970s "there has been a sense of crisis about what it has meant to be British",[[133]](#cite_note-Ward1-137) exacerbated by growing demands for greater political autonomy for [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), and [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales").[[134]](#cite_note-Spohn25-138)

The late 20th century saw major changes to the [politics of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Politics_of_the_United_Kingdom "Politics of the United Kingdom") with the establishment of [devolved](/wiki/Devolution "Devolution") national administrations for Northern Ireland, Scotland, and Wales following pre-legislative [referendums](/wiki/Referendums_in_the_United_Kingdom "Referendums in the United Kingdom").[[135]](#cite_note-139) Calls for greater autonomy for the four [countries of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Countries of the United Kingdom") had existed since their original union with each other, but gathered pace in the 1960s and 1970s.[[134]](#cite_note-Spohn25-138) Devolution has led to "increasingly assertive Scottish, Welsh and Irish national identities",[[136]](#cite_note-Ward180-140) resulting in more diverse cultural expressions of Britishness,[[137]](#cite_note-141) or else its outright rejection: [Gwynfor Evans](/wiki/Gwynfor_Evans "Gwynfor Evans"), a [Welsh nationalist](/wiki/Welsh_nationalism "Welsh nationalism") politician active in the late 20th century, rebuffed Britishness as "a political synonym for Englishness which extends English culture over the Scots, Welsh and the Irish".[[138]](#cite_note-142)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Ve_Day_Celebrations_in_London%2C_England%2C_UK%2C_8_May_1945_D24587.jpg/250px-Ve_Day_Celebrations_in_London%2C_England%2C_UK%2C_8_May_1945_D24587.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ve_Day_Celebrations_in_London,_England,_UK,_8_May_1945_D24587.jpg)

Britons gathered in [Whitehall](/wiki/Whitehall "Whitehall") to hear [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill")'s victory speech on 8 May 1945.

In 2004, Sir [Bernard Crick](/wiki/Bernard_Crick "Bernard Crick"), political theorist and [democratic socialist](/wiki/Democratic_socialism "Democratic socialism") tasked with developing the [life in the United Kingdom test](/wiki/Life_in_the_United_Kingdom_test "Life in the United Kingdom test") said:

> Britishness, to me, is an overarching political and legal concept: it signifies allegiance to the laws, government and broad moral and political concepts—like tolerance and freedom of expression—that hold the United Kingdom together.[[139]](#cite_note-Brad34-143)[[140]](#cite_note-144)

[Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown") initiated a debate on British identity in 2006.[[141]](#cite_note-Brown-145) Brown's speech to the [Fabian Society](/wiki/Fabian_Society "Fabian Society")'s Britishness Conference proposed that British values demand a new constitutional settlement and symbols to represent a modern patriotism, including a new youth community service scheme and a [British Day](/wiki/British_Day "British Day") to celebrate.[[141]](#cite_note-Brown-145) One of the central issues identified at the Fabian Society conference was how the English identity fits within the framework of a devolved United Kingdom.[[141]](#cite_note-Brown-145) An expression of [Her Majesty's Government](/wiki/Her_Majesty%27s_Government "Her Majesty's Government")'s initiative to promote Britishness was the inaugural [Veterans' Day](/wiki/Veterans%27_Day_(United_Kingdom) "Veterans' Day (United Kingdom)") which was first held on 27 June 2006. As well as celebrating the achievements of armed forces veterans, Brown's speech at the first event for the celebration said:

> Scots and people from the rest of the UK share the purpose that Britain has something to say to the rest of the world about the values of freedom, democracy and the dignity of the people that you stand up for. So at a time when people can talk about football and devolution and money, it is important that we also remember the values that we share in common.[[142]](#cite_note-146)

In 2018, the [Windrush scandal](/wiki/Windrush_scandal "Windrush scandal") illustrated complex developments in British peoplehood, when it was revealed hundreds of Britons had been wrongfully deported.[[143]](#cite_note-147) With roots in the break-up of the empire, and post-war rebuilding; the [Windrush generation](/wiki/Windrush_generation "Windrush generation") had arrived as CUKC citizens in the 1950s and 1960s. Born in former [British colonies](/wiki/British_colonies "British colonies"), they settled in the UK before 1973, and were granted "right of abode" by the [Immigration Act 1971](/wiki/Immigration_Act_1971 "Immigration Act 1971").[[28]](#cite_note-windrushreport2020-32) Having faced removal, or been deported, many British people of [African Caribbean](/wiki/African_Caribbean "African Caribbean") heritage suffered with loss of home, livelihood, and health.[[28]](#cite_note-windrushreport2020-32) As a result of the political scandal, many institutions and elected politicians publicly affirmed that these individuals, while not legally holding British citizenship or nationality, were, in fact, British people. These included British Prime Minister [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May"),[[144]](#cite_note-148) London Mayor [Sadiq Khan](/wiki/Sadiq_Khan "Sadiq Khan"),[[145]](#cite_note-149) Her Majesty's [CPS Inspectorate](/wiki/Her_Majesty%27s_Crown_Prosecution_Service_Inspectorate "Her Majesty's Crown Prosecution Service Inspectorate") Wendy Williams and her [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom")-ordered *Windrush Lessons Learned Review*,[[146]](#cite_note-150)[[147]](#cite_note-151) the [Chartered Institute of Housing](/wiki/Chartered_Institute_of_Housing "Chartered Institute of Housing"),[[28]](#cite_note-windrushreport2020-32) [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International"),[[148]](#cite_note-152) [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford")'s social geographer [Danny Dorling](/wiki/Danny_Dorling "Danny Dorling"),[[149]](#cite_note-153) and other public figures.[[150]](#cite_note-154)[[151]](#cite_note-155)

## Geographic distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geographic distribution")]

Main article: [British diaspora](/wiki/British_diaspora "British diaspora")

See also: [English-speaking world](/wiki/English-speaking_world "English-speaking world"), [List of countries by British immigrants](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_British_immigrants "List of countries by British immigrants"), and [Commonwealth diaspora](/wiki/Commonwealth_diaspora "Commonwealth diaspora")

The earliest migrations of Britons date from the 5th and 6th centuries AD, when Brittonic Celts fleeing the Anglo-Saxon invasions migrated what is today northern France and north western Spain and forged the colonies of [Brittany](/wiki/Brittany "Brittany") and [Britonia](/wiki/Britonia "Britonia"). Brittany remained independent of France until the early 16th century and still retains a distinct Brittonic culture and language, whilst Britonia in modern [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Spain) "Galicia (Spain)") was absorbed into Spanish states by the end of the 9th century AD.

Britons – people with British citizenship or of British descent – have a significant presence in a number of countries other than the United Kingdom, and in particular in those with historic connections to the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"). After the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery"), the British were one of the earliest and largest communities to emigrate out of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), and the British Empire's expansion during the first half of the 19th century triggered an "extraordinary dispersion of the British people", resulting in particular concentrations "in [Australasia](/wiki/Australasia "Australasia") and [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America")".[[152]](#cite_note-BritDis47-156)

The British Empire was "built on waves of migration overseas by British people",[[153]](#cite_note-Marsh254-157) who left the United Kingdom and "reached across the globe and permanently affected population structures in three continents".[[152]](#cite_note-BritDis47-156) As a result of the [British colonisation of the Americas](/wiki/British_colonisation_of_the_Americas "British colonisation of the Americas"), what became the United States was "easily the greatest single destination of emigrant British", but in Australia the British experienced a [birth rate](/wiki/Birth_rate "Birth rate") higher than "anything seen before", resulting in the displacement of [indigenous Australians](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians").[[152]](#cite_note-BritDis47-156)

In colonies such as [Southern Rhodesia](/wiki/Southern_Rhodesia "Southern Rhodesia"), [British East Africa](/wiki/British_East_Africa "British East Africa") and [Cape Colony](/wiki/Cape_Colony "Cape Colony"), permanently resident British communities were established and, whilst never more than a numerical minority, these Britons "exercised a dominant influence" upon the culture and politics of those lands.[[153]](#cite_note-Marsh254-157) In Australia, Canada and New Zealand, "people of British origin came to constitute the majority of the population", contributing to these states becoming integral to the [Anglosphere](/wiki/Anglosphere "Anglosphere").[[153]](#cite_note-Marsh254-157)

The [1861 United Kingdom census](/wiki/1861_United_Kingdom_census "1861 United Kingdom census") estimated the size of the overseas British to be around 2.5 million, but concluded that most of these were "not conventional settlers" but rather "travellers, merchants, professionals, and military personnel".[[152]](#cite_note-BritDis47-156) By 1890, there were over 1.5 million further UK-born people living in Australia, Canada, New Zealand and [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa").[[152]](#cite_note-BritDis47-156) A 2006 publication from the [Institute for Public Policy Research](/wiki/Institute_for_Public_Policy_Research "Institute for Public Policy Research") estimated 5.6 million Britons lived outside of the United Kingdom.[[8]](#cite_note-BritsAbroad-12)[[154]](#cite_note-158)

Outside of the United Kingdom and its [Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories"), up to 76% of [Australians](/wiki/Australians "Australians"), 70% of [New Zealanders](/wiki/New_Zealanders "New Zealanders"), 48% of [Canadians](/wiki/Canadians "Canadians"), 33% of [Americans](/wiki/Americans "Americans"), 4% of [Chileans](/wiki/Chileans "Chileans") and 3% of [South Africans](/wiki/South_Africans "South Africans") have ancestry from the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles").[[155]](#cite_note-Australia2-159)[[12]](#cite_note-NewZealand1-16)[[10]](#cite_note-BritCan-14)[[4]](#cite_note-2020USA-6)[[156]](#cite_note-biografiadechile.cl-160)[[13]](#cite_note-Census2011-17) Hong Kong has the highest proportion of British nationals outside of the United Kingdom and its Overseas Territories, with 47% of Hong Kong residents holding a British National (Overseas) status or a British citizenship.[[157]](#cite_note-auto1-161) The next highest concentrations of British citizens outside of the United Kingdom and its Overseas Territories are located in Barbados (10%), the Republic of Ireland (7%), Australia (6%) and New Zealand (5%).[[8]](#cite_note-BritsAbroad-12)

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Australia")]

See also: [First Fleet](/wiki/First_Fleet "First Fleet") and [Anglo-Celtic Australian](/wiki/Anglo-Celtic_Australian "Anglo-Celtic Australian")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/b9/Flag_of_Australia.svg/250px-Flag_of_Australia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Australia.svg)

The [flag of Australia](/wiki/Flag_of_Australia "Flag of Australia") was approved by Australian and British authorities, and features a [Union Flag](/wiki/Union_Flag "Union Flag")—the [flag of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom "Flag of the United Kingdom")—in the canton. Australia has one of the largest concentrations of people of British heritage.

From the [beginning of Australia's colonial period](/wiki/History_of_Australia_(1788%E2%80%931850) "History of Australia (1788–1850)") until after the Second World War, people from the United Kingdom made up a large majority of people coming to Australia, meaning that many people born in Australia can trace their origins to Britain.[[158]](#cite_note-ausbrit-162) The colony of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), founded on 26 January 1788, was part of the eastern half of Australia claimed by the Kingdom of Great Britain in 1770, and initially settled by Britons through [penal transportation](/wiki/Penal_transportation "Penal transportation"). Together with another five largely self-governing Crown Colonies, the [federation of Australia](/wiki/Federation_of_Australia "Federation of Australia") was achieved on 1 January 1901.

Its history of British dominance meant that Australia was "grounded in British culture and political traditions that had been transported to the Australian colonies in the nineteenth century and become part of colonial culture and politics".[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalligan_et_al._2001113-163) Australia maintains the [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system") of parliamentary government and [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") as [King of Australia](/wiki/King_of_Australia "King of Australia"). Until 1987, the national status of Australian citizens was formally described as "British Subject: Citizen of Australia". Britons continue to make up a substantial proportion of immigrants.[[158]](#cite_note-ausbrit-162)

By 1947, Australia was fundamentally British in origin with 7,524,129 or 99.3% of the population declaring themselves as European.[[160]](#cite_note-164) In the [2016 census](/wiki/2016_Australian_census "2016 Australian census"), a large proportion of Australians self-identified with British ancestral origins, including 36.1% or 7,852,224 as [English](/wiki/English_Australians "English Australians") and 9.3% (2,023,474) as [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_Australians "Scottish Australians") alone.[[161]](#cite_note-165)[[162]](#cite_note-166) A substantial proportion —33.5%— chose to identify as 'Australian', the census Bureau has stated that most of these are of [Anglo-Celtic](/wiki/Anglo-Celtic_Australians "Anglo-Celtic Australians") colonial stock.[[163]](#cite_note-167) All [six states of Australia](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_Australia "States and territories of Australia") retain the Union Jack in the canton of their respective flags.

### British Overseas Territories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: British Overseas Territories")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Colour_party_of_the_Royal_Bermuda_Regiment_at_Queen%27s_Birthday_Parade_in_2017.jpg/250px-Colour_party_of_the_Royal_Bermuda_Regiment_at_Queen%27s_Birthday_Parade_in_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Colour_party_of_the_Royal_Bermuda_Regiment_at_Queen%27s_Birthday_Parade_in_2017.jpg)

Colour party of the [Royal Bermuda Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Bermuda_Regiment "Royal Bermuda Regiment") at the [Queen's Birthday](/wiki/King%27s_Official_Birthday "King's Official Birthday") Parade in [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda") on 10 June 2017

The approximately 250,000 people of the [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories") are British by [citizenship](/wiki/Citizenship "Citizenship"), via origins or [naturalisation](/wiki/Naturalisation "Naturalisation"). Along with aspects of common British identity, each of the territories has its own distinct identity shaped in its own political, economic, ethnic, social and cultural history. For instance, in the case of the [Falkland Islanders](/wiki/Falkland_Islanders "Falkland Islanders"), a former [Speaker](/wiki/Speaker_(politics) "Speaker (politics)") of the [Legislative Council of the Falkland Islands](/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_the_Falkland_Islands "Legislative Council of the Falkland Islands"), Lewis Clifton, explains:

> British cultural, economic, social, political and educational values create a unique British-like, Falkland Islands. Yet Islanders feel distinctly different from their fellow citizens who reside in the United Kingdom. This might have something to do with geographical isolation or with living on a smaller island—perhaps akin to those Britons not feeling European.[[164]](#cite_note-168)

In contrast, for the majority of the [Gibraltarians](/wiki/Gibraltarians "Gibraltarians"), who live in [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar"), there is an "insistence on their Britishness" which "carries excessive loyalty" to Britain.[[165]](#cite_note-inde-169) The sovereignty of Gibraltar has been a point of contention in [Spain–United Kingdom relations](/wiki/Spain%E2%80%93United_Kingdom_relations "Spain–United Kingdom relations"), but an overwhelming number of Gibraltarians embrace Britishness with strong conviction, in direct opposition to [Spanish territorial claims](/wiki/Disputed_status_of_Gibraltar "Disputed status of Gibraltar").[[165]](#cite_note-inde-169)[[166]](#cite_note-170)[[167]](#cite_note-171)

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Canada")]

See also: [Canadians](/wiki/Canadians "Canadians")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Flag_of_Canada_%281921%E2%80%931957%29.svg/250px-Flag_of_Canada_%281921%E2%80%931957%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Canada_(1921%E2%80%931957).svg)

The [Canadian Red Ensign](/wiki/Canadian_Red_Ensign "Canadian Red Ensign") was the flag of Canada pre-1965, and features a [Union Flag](/wiki/Union_Flag "Union Flag")—the [flag of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom "Flag of the United Kingdom")—in the canton. Canada has a large concentration of people of British heritage.

Canada traces its statehood to the [French](/wiki/French_colonisation_of_the_Americas "French colonisation of the Americas"), [English, and Scottish](/wiki/British_colonisation_of_the_Americas "British colonisation of the Americas") expeditions of North America from the late-15th century. France ceded nearly all of [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France") in 1763 after the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"), and so after the [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") in 1776, [Quebec](/wiki/Province_of_Quebec_(1763%E2%80%931791) "Province of Quebec (1763–1791)") and [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia") formed "the nucleus of the colonies that constituted Britain's remaining stake on the North American continent".[[168]](#cite_note-Marsh34-172) [British North America](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America") (the archipelago of [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda") and those continental colonies remaining after the independence of the thirteen that would become the United States) attracted the [United Empire Loyalists](/wiki/United_Empire_Loyalist "United Empire Loyalist"), Britons who migrated out of what they considered the "rebellious" United States, increasing the size of British communities in what was to become Canada.[[168]](#cite_note-Marsh34-172)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/TimbrePosteCanada5centsElizabethIIWilding1954.jpg/250px-TimbrePosteCanada5centsElizabethIIWilding1954.jpg)](/wiki/File:TimbrePosteCanada5centsElizabethIIWilding1954.jpg)

Postage stamp with portrait of Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II"), 1954

In 1867 there was a union of three colonies of British North America, the [Province of Canada](/wiki/Province_of_Canada "Province of Canada"), [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"), and [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick"), which together formed the [Canadian Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation"), a [federal](/wiki/Federalism "Federalism") [dominion](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion").[[169]](#cite_note-173)[[170]](#cite_note-174)[[171]](#cite_note-175) This began an [accretion of additional provinces and territories](/wiki/Territorial_evolution_of_Canada "Territorial evolution of Canada") and a process of increasing autonomy from the United Kingdom, highlighted by the [Statute of Westminster 1931](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931") and culminating in the [Canada Act 1982](/wiki/Canada_Act_1982 "Canada Act 1982"), which severed the vestiges of legal dependence on the [parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"). Nevertheless, it is recognised that there is a "continuing importance of Canada's long and close relationship with Britain";[[172]](#cite_note-176) large parts of Canada's modern population claim "British origins" and the British cultural impact upon Canada's institutions is profound.[[173]](#cite_note-177)

It was not until 1977 that the phrase "A Canadian citizen is a British subject" ceased to be used in Canadian passports. The [politics of Canada](/wiki/Politics_of_Canada "Politics of Canada") are strongly influenced by British political culture.[[174]](#cite_note-178)[[175]](#cite_note-MacLeod17-179) Although significant modifications have been made, Canada is governed by a democratic parliamentary framework comparable to the [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system"), and retains [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") as [King of Canada](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada") and head of state.[[176]](#cite_note-180)[[177]](#cite_note-Buck1-181) English is the most commonly spoken language used in Canada and it is an official language of Canada.[[178]](#cite_note-182)

British iconography remains present in the design of many [Canadian flags](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_flags "List of Canadian flags"), with 10 out of 13 Canadian provincial and territorial flags adopting some form of British symbolism in their design. The Union Jack is also an official ceremonial flag in Canada, known as the Royal Union Flag, which is flown outside of federal buildings three days of the year.[[179]](#cite_note-DCH-183)[[180]](#cite_note-ruf-184)

### New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: New Zealand")]

See also: [New Zealand European](/wiki/New_Zealand_European "New Zealand European") and [Immigration to New Zealand](/wiki/Immigration_to_New_Zealand "Immigration to New Zealand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Flag_of_New_Zealand.svg/250px-Flag_of_New_Zealand.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_New_Zealand.svg)

The [flag of New Zealand](/wiki/Flag_of_New_Zealand "Flag of New Zealand") features a [Union Flag](/wiki/Union_Flag "Union Flag")—the [flag of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom "Flag of the United Kingdom")—in the canton. [A referendum held in 2016](/wiki/2015%E2%80%932016_New_Zealand_flag_referendums "2015–2016 New Zealand flag referendums") found that 57% of New Zealand voters wanted to retain the current design of the New Zealand flag.

As a long-term result of [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook")'s voyage of 1768–1771,[[181]](#cite_note-MeinSmith23-185) a significant number of New Zealanders are of British descent, for whom a sense of Britishness has contributed to their identity.[[182]](#cite_note-186) As late as the 1950s, it was common for British New Zealanders to refer to themselves as British, such as when Prime Minister [Keith Holyoake](/wiki/Keith_Holyoake "Keith Holyoake") described Sir [Edmund Hillary](/wiki/Edmund_Hillary "Edmund Hillary")'s successful ascent of [Mount Everest](/wiki/Mount_Everest "Mount Everest") as putting "the British race and New Zealand on top of the world".[[183]](#cite_note-187) [New Zealand passports](/wiki/New_Zealand_passport "New Zealand passport") described nationals as "British Subject: Citizen of New Zealand" until 1974, when this was changed to "New Zealand citizen".[[184]](#cite_note-188)

In an interview with the *[New Zealand Listener](/wiki/New_Zealand_Listener "New Zealand Listener")* in 2006, [Don Brash](/wiki/Don_Brash "Don Brash"), the then [Leader of the Opposition](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Opposition_(New_Zealand) "Leader of the Opposition (New Zealand)"), said:

> British immigrants fit in here very well. My own ancestry is all British. New Zealand values are British values, derived from centuries of struggle since Magna Carta. Those things make New Zealand the society it is.[[185]](#cite_note-189)

The [politics of New Zealand](/wiki/Politics_of_New_Zealand "Politics of New Zealand") are strongly influenced by British political culture. Although significant modifications have been made, New Zealand is governed by a democratic parliamentary framework comparable to the [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system"), and it retains [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") as the head of the [monarchy of New Zealand](/wiki/Monarchy_of_New_Zealand "Monarchy of New Zealand").[[186]](#cite_note-190) English is the dominant official language used in New Zealand.[[187]](#cite_note-191)

### Hong Kong

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Hong Kong")]

See also: [British Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong"), [British nationality law and Hong Kong](/wiki/British_nationality_law_and_Hong_Kong "British nationality law and Hong Kong"), [British National (Overseas)](/wiki/British_National_(Overseas) "British National (Overseas)"), [Britons in Hong Kong](/wiki/Britons_in_Hong_Kong "Britons in Hong Kong"), and [Handover of Hong Kong](/wiki/Handover_of_Hong_Kong "Handover of Hong Kong")

British nationality law as it pertains to [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") has been unusual ever since Hong Kong became a [British colony](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong") in 1842.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETsang200412-192) From its beginning as a sparsely populated trading port to its modern role as a cosmopolitan international financial centre of over seven million people, the territory has attracted refugees, immigrants and expatriates alike searching for a new life.[[189]](#cite_note-193) Citizenship matters were complicated by the fact that [British nationality law](/wiki/British_nationality_law "British nationality law") treated those born in Hong Kong as [British subjects](/wiki/British_subjects "British subjects") ([Citizens of the United Kingdom and Colonies](/wiki/Citizens_of_the_United_Kingdom_and_Colonies "Citizens of the United Kingdom and Colonies") from 1948) based on the principle of *jus soli*, while the People's Republic of China (PRC) did not recognise the [ethnically Chinese](/wiki/Ethnically_Chinese "Ethnically Chinese") population in Hong Kong as such.[[190]](#cite_note-LEGISLATION.GO-194)[[191]](#cite_note-original_act_doc-195)

The main reason was that recognising these British-born nationals would have been seen as a tacit acceptance of a series of historical treaties labelled by the PRC as "unequal", including the ones that ceded [Hong Kong Island](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Island "Hong Kong Island") (*Treaty of Nanking*) and [Kowloon Peninsula](/wiki/Kowloon_Peninsula "Kowloon Peninsula") (*Convention of Peking*) to Britain and the [New Territories](/wiki/New_Territories "New Territories") lease. The British government, however, recognising the unique political situation of Hong Kong, granted 3.4 million [Hongkongers](/wiki/Hongkongers "Hongkongers") a new class of British nationality known as [British National (Overseas)](/wiki/British_National_(Overseas) "British National (Overseas)"), which is established in accordance with the *[Hong Kong Act 1985](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Act_1985 "Hong Kong Act 1985")*.[[192]](#cite_note-196) Some of those also have British citizenship in conjunction with their British National (Overseas) citizenship. Both British Nationals (Overseas) and British citizens are British nationals and [Commonwealth citizens](/wiki/Commonwealth_citizen "Commonwealth citizen") according to the British Nationality Law, which enables them to various rights in the United Kingdom.

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: United States")]

Further information: [British Americans](/wiki/British_Americans "British Americans")

See also: [British colonisation of the Americas](/wiki/British_colonisation_of_the_Americas "British colonisation of the Americas"), [British America](/wiki/British_America "British America"), and [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England")

An English presence in North America began with the [Roanoke Colony](/wiki/Roanoke_Colony "Roanoke Colony") and [Colony of Virginia](/wiki/Colony_of_Virginia "Colony of Virginia") in the late-16th century, but the first successful English settlement was established in 1607, on the [James River](/wiki/James_River "James River") at [Jamestown](/wiki/Jamestown,_Virginia "Jamestown, Virginia"). By the 1610s an estimated 1,300 English people had travelled to North America, the "first of many millions from the British Isles".[[193]](#cite_note-BritDis48-197) In 1620, the [Pilgrims](/wiki/Pilgrim_(Plymouth_Colony) "Pilgrim (Plymouth Colony)") established the English imperial venture of [Plymouth Colony](/wiki/Plymouth_Colony "Plymouth Colony"), beginning "a remarkable acceleration of permanent emigration from England" with over 60% of trans-Atlantic English migrants settling in the [New England Colonies](/wiki/New_England_Colonies "New England Colonies").[[193]](#cite_note-BritDis48-197) During the 17th century, an estimated 350,000 English and Welsh migrants arrived in North America, which in the century after the [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") was surpassed in rate and number by Scottish and Irish migrants.[[194]](#cite_note-BritDis49-198)
