The British policy of [salutary neglect](/wiki/Salutary_neglect "Salutary neglect") for its North American colonies intended to minimise trade restrictions as a way of ensuring that they stayed loyal to British interests.[[195]](#cite_note-history_of_colonial-199) This permitted the development of the [American Dream](/wiki/American_Dream "American Dream"), a cultural spirit distinct from that of its European founders.[[195]](#cite_note-history_of_colonial-199) The [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") of [British America](/wiki/British_America "British America") began an armed rebellion against British rule in 1775 when they rejected the [right](/wiki/Rights_of_Englishmen "Rights of Englishmen") of the [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain") to govern them [without representation](/wiki/No_taxation_without_representation "No taxation without representation"); they proclaimed their independence in 1776, and constituted the first thirteen states of the United States of America, which became a [sovereign state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state") in 1781 with the ratification of the [Articles of Confederation](/wiki/Articles_of_Confederation "Articles of Confederation"). The [1783 Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)") represented Great Britain's formal acknowledgement of the United States' sovereignty at the end of the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War").[[196]](#cite_note-road-200)

Nevertheless, longstanding cultural and historical ties have, in more modern times, resulted in the [Special Relationship](/wiki/Special_Relationship "Special Relationship"), the historically close political, diplomatic, and military co-operation between the [United Kingdom and United States](/wiki/United_Kingdom_%E2%80%93_United_States_relations "United Kingdom – United States relations").[[197]](#cite_note-wither-201) [Linda Colley](/wiki/Linda_Colley "Linda Colley"), a professor of history at [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University") and specialist in Britishness, suggested that because of their colonial influence on the United States, the British find Americans a "mysterious and paradoxical people, physically distant but culturally close, engagingly similar yet irritatingly different".[[198]](#cite_note-202)

For over two centuries of early U.S. history, all [Presidents](/wiki/American_presidents "American presidents") with the exception of two (Van Buren and Kennedy) were descended from the varied colonial British stock, from the Pilgrims and Puritans to the Scotch-Irish and English who settled the [Appalachia](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia").[[199]](#cite_note-203) The largest concentrations of self-reported British ethnic ancestry in the United States were found to be in [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah") (35%), [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine") (30%), [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire") (25%) and [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont") (25%) at the 2015 American Community Survey.[[200]](#cite_note-204) Overall, 10.7% of Americans reported their ethnic ancestry as some form of "British" in the 2013–17 ACS, behind [German](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans") and [African](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans") ancestries and on par with [Mexican](/wiki/Mexican_Americans "Mexican Americans") and [Irish](/wiki/Irish_Americans "Irish Americans") ancestries.[[201]](#cite_note-BritUS-205)

### Chile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Chile")]

Main article: [British Chilean](/wiki/British_Chilean "British Chilean")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Antofagasta.jpg/250px-Antofagasta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Antofagasta.jpg)

British and Chilean flags in a monument in [Antofagasta](/wiki/Antofagasta "Antofagasta") city

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Escudo_de_Coquimbo.svg/120px-Escudo_de_Coquimbo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Escudo_de_Coquimbo.svg)

Coat of arms of [Coquimbo](/wiki/Coquimbo "Coquimbo"), with the Union Flag

Approximately 4% of [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile")'s population is of British or Irish descent.[[202]](#cite_note-206) Over 50,000[[203]](#cite_note-207) British [immigrants settled in Chile](/wiki/Immigration_to_Chile "Immigration to Chile") from 1840 to 1914. A significant number of them settled in [Magallanes Province](/wiki/Magallanes_Province "Magallanes Province"), especially in the city of [Punta Arenas](/wiki/Punta_Arenas,_Chile "Punta Arenas, Chile") when it flourished as a major global seaport for ships crossing between the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans through the Strait of Magellan. Around 32,000 English settled in [Valparaíso](/wiki/Valpara%C3%ADso "Valparaíso"), influencing the port city to the extent of making it virtually a British colony during the last decades of the 19th century and the beginning of the 20th century.[[204]](#cite_note-galeon-208) However, the opening of the [Panama Canal](/wiki/Panama_Canal "Panama Canal") in 1914 and the outbreak of the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") drove many of them away from the city or back to Europe.

In [Valparaíso](/wiki/Valpara%C3%ADso "Valparaíso"), they created their largest and most important colony, bringing with them neighbourhoods of British character, schools, [social clubs](/wiki/Social_clubs "Social clubs"), [sports clubs](/wiki/Sports_clubs "Sports clubs"), [business organisations](/wiki/Business_organisations "Business organisations") and [periodicals](/wiki/Periodicals "Periodicals"). Even today, their influence is apparent in specific areas, such as the banks and the navy, as well as in certain social activities, such as [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), horse racing and the custom of drinking tea.

During the movement for Chilean [independence](/wiki/Independence "Independence") (1818), it was mainly the British who formed the [Chilean Navy](/wiki/Chilean_Navy "Chilean Navy"), under the command of [Lord Cochrane](/wiki/Thomas_Cochrane,_10th_Earl_of_Dundonald "Thomas Cochrane, 10th Earl of Dundonald"). British investment helped Chile become prosperous and British seamen helped the Chilean navy become a strong force in the South Pacific. Chile won two wars, the first against the Peru-Bolivian Confederation, and the second, the [War of the Pacific](/wiki/War_of_the_Pacific "War of the Pacific"), in 1878–79, against an alliance between [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru") and [Bolivia](/wiki/Bolivia "Bolivia"). The liberal-socialist "Revolution of 1891" introduced political reforms modelled on British parliamentary practice and lawmaking.

British immigrants were also important in the northern zone of the country during the [saltpetre](/wiki/Saltpetre "Saltpetre") boom, in the ports of [Iquique](/wiki/Iquique "Iquique") and [Pisagua](/wiki/Pisagua,_Chile "Pisagua, Chile"). The "King of Saltpetre", [John Thomas North](/wiki/John_Thomas_North "John Thomas North"), was the principal tycoon of nitrate mining. The British legacy is reflected in the streets of the historic district of the city of Iquique, with the foundation of various institutions, such as the [Club Hípico](/wiki/Club_Hipico_de_Santiago "Club Hipico de Santiago") (Racing Club). Nevertheless, the British active presence came to an end with the saltpetre crisis during the 1930s.

Some [Scots](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") settled in the country's more temperate regions, where the climate and the forested landscape with glaciers and islands may have reminded them of their homeland (the Highlands and Northern Scotland), while [English](/wiki/English_Chilean "English Chilean") and [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_Chilean "Welsh Chilean") made up the rest. The Irish immigrants, who were frequently confused with the British, arrived as [merchants](/wiki/Merchants "Merchants"), [tradesmen](/wiki/Tradesmen "Tradesmen") and sailors, settling along with the British in the main trading cities and ports. An important contingent of British (principally Welsh) immigrants arrived between 1914 and 1950, settling in the present-day region of [Magallanes](/wiki/Magallanes_y_la_Ant%C3%A1rtica_Chilena_Region "Magallanes y la Antártica Chilena Region"). British families were established in other areas of the country, such as [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago,_Chile "Santiago, Chile"), [Coquimbo](/wiki/Coquimbo "Coquimbo"), the [Araucanía](/wiki/Araucan%C3%ADa_Region "Araucanía Region") and [Chiloé](/wiki/Chilo%C3%A9 "Chiloé").

The cultural legacy of the British in Chile is notable and has spread beyond the British Chilean community into society at large. Customs taken from the British include [afternoon tea](/wiki/Afternoon_tea "Afternoon tea") (called [onces](/wiki/Tea "Tea") by Chileans), [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") and [horse racing](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing"). Another legacy is the widespread use of British personal names by Chileans. Chile has the largest population of descendants of British settlers in Latin America. Over 700,000 Chileans may have British (English, [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") and [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_people "Welsh people")) origin, amounting to 4.5% of Chile's population.[[205]](#cite_note-british-209)

### South Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: South Africa")]

Further information: [British diaspora in Africa](/wiki/British_diaspora_in_Africa "British diaspora in Africa") and [1820 Settlers](/wiki/1820_Settlers "1820 Settlers")

See also: [White South Africans](/wiki/White_South_Africans "White South Africans")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Cecil_Rhodes_-_Project_Gutenberg_eText_16600.jpg/250px-Cecil_Rhodes_-_Project_Gutenberg_eText_16600.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cecil_Rhodes_-_Project_Gutenberg_eText_16600.jpg)

[Cecil John Rhodes](/wiki/Cecil_Rhodes "Cecil Rhodes"), the 6th [Prime Minister of the Cape Colony](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_Cape_Colony "Prime Minister of the Cape Colony") (divided between two provinces in modern-day South Africa) and founder of the [De Beers](/wiki/De_Beers "De Beers") diamond company

The British arrived in the area which would become the modern-day [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") during the early 18th century, yet substantial settlement only started end of the 18th century, in the [Cape of Good Hope](/wiki/Cape_of_Good_Hope "Cape of Good Hope").[[206]](#cite_note-210) In the late 19th century, the discovery of gold and diamonds further encouraged colonisation of South Africa by the British, and the population of the British-South Africans rose substantially, although there was fierce rivalry between the British and [Afrikaners](/wiki/Afrikaners "Afrikaners") (descendants of Dutch colonists) in the period known as the [Boer Wars](/wiki/Boer_Wars "Boer Wars"). The latest census in South Africa showed that there are almost 2 million British-South Africans; they make up about 40% of the total [White South African](/wiki/White_South_African "White South African") demographic, and the greatest white British ancestry populations in South Africa are in the [KwaZulu-Natal](/wiki/KwaZulu-Natal "KwaZulu-Natal") province and in the cities of [Cape Town](/wiki/Cape_Town "Cape Town"), [Durban](/wiki/Durban "Durban") and [Port Elizabeth](/wiki/Port_Elizabeth "Port Elizabeth").[[207]](#cite_note-Census-211)

### Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Ireland")]

Further information: [Plantations of Ireland](/wiki/Plantations_of_Ireland "Plantations of Ireland"), [Unionism in Ireland](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland"), and [The Troubles](/wiki/The_Troubles "The Troubles")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/The_Special_Air_Service_during_the_Second_World_War_MH24415.jpg/250px-The_Special_Air_Service_during_the_Second_World_War_MH24415.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Special_Air_Service_during_the_Second_World_War_MH24415.jpg)

[Paddy Mayne](/wiki/Paddy_Mayne "Paddy Mayne") from [County Down](/wiki/County_Down "County Down"); a founding member of the [SAS](/wiki/Special_Air_Service "Special Air Service"); was one of the most decorated British soldiers of World War II. He also played rugby for Ireland.

Plantations of Ireland introduced large numbers of people from Great Britain to [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland") throughout the Middle Ages and [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period"). The resulting [Protestant Ascendancy](/wiki/Protestant_Ascendancy "Protestant Ascendancy"), the aristocratic class of the [Lordship of Ireland](/wiki/Lordship_of_Ireland "Lordship of Ireland"), broadly identified themselves as [Anglo-Irish](/wiki/Anglo-Irish "Anglo-Irish").[[208]](#cite_note-212) In the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries, Protestant British settlers subjugated Catholic, Gaelic inhabitants in the north of Ireland during the [Plantation of Ulster](/wiki/Plantation_of_Ulster "Plantation of Ulster") and the [Williamite War in Ireland](/wiki/Williamite_War_in_Ireland "Williamite War in Ireland"); it was "an explicit attempt to control Ireland strategically by introducing ethnic and religious elements loyal to the British interest in Ireland".[[209]](#cite_note-Craith169-213)

The [Ulster Scots people](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_people "Ulster Scots people") are an ethnic group of British origin in Ireland, broadly descended from [Lowland Scots](/wiki/Scottish_Lowlands "Scottish Lowlands") who settled in large numbers in the province of [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster") during the planned process of [colonisations](/wiki/Colonisation "Colonisation") of Ireland which took place in the reign of James VI of Scotland and I of England. Together with English and Welsh settlers, these Scots introduced Protestantism (particularly the [Presbyterianism](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism") of the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland")) and the [Ulster Scots](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_dialects "Ulster Scots dialects") and [English languages](/wiki/English_language "English language") to, mainly, northeastern Ireland. With the partition of Ireland and independence for what is now the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") some of these people found themselves no longer living within the United Kingdom.

Northern Ireland itself was, for many years, the site of a violent and bitter ethno-sectarian conflict—[The Troubles](/wiki/The_Troubles "The Troubles")—between those claiming to represent [Irish nationalism](/wiki/Irish_nationalism "Irish nationalism"), who are predominantly [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic"), and those claiming to represent [British unionism](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland"), who are predominantly [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant").[[210]](#cite_note-214) Unionists want Northern Ireland to remain part of the United Kingdom,[[211]](#cite_note-215) while nationalists desire a [united Ireland](/wiki/United_Ireland "United Ireland").[[212]](#cite_note-216)[[213]](#cite_note-217)

Since the signing of the [Good Friday Agreement](/wiki/Good_Friday_Agreement "Good Friday Agreement") in 1998, most of the paramilitary groups involved in the Troubles have ceased their armed campaigns, and constitutionally, the [people of Northern Ireland](/wiki/People_of_Northern_Ireland "People of Northern Ireland") have been recognised as "all persons born in Northern Ireland and having, at the time of their birth, at least one parent who is a British citizen, an Irish citizen or is otherwise entitled to reside in Northern Ireland without any restriction on their period of residence".[[214]](#cite_note-GFA-218) The Good Friday Agreement guarantees the "recognition of the birthright of all the people of Northern Ireland to identify themselves and be accepted as Irish or British, or both, as they may so choose".[[214]](#cite_note-GFA-218)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Culture")]

See also: [Culture of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Culture_of_the_United_Kingdom "Culture of the United Kingdom")

Result from the expansion of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), British cultural influence can be observed in the language and culture of a geographically wide assortment of countries such as Canada, Australia, New Zealand, [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), India, [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), the United States, and the [British overseas territories](/wiki/British_overseas_territory "British overseas territory"). These states are sometimes collectively known as the [Anglosphere](/wiki/Anglosphere "Anglosphere").[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBennett200480-219) As well as the British influence on its empire, the empire also influenced British culture, particularly [British cuisine](/wiki/British_cuisine "British cuisine"). Innovations and movements within the wider-[culture of Europe](/wiki/Culture_of_Europe "Culture of Europe") have also changed the United Kingdom; [Humanism](/wiki/Humanism "Humanism"), [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"), and [representative democracy](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy") have developed from broader [Western culture](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture"). As a result of the [history of the formation of the United Kingdom](/wiki/History_of_the_formation_of_the_United_Kingdom "History of the formation of the United Kingdom"), the [cultures of England](/wiki/Culture_of_England "Culture of England"), [Scotland](/wiki/Culture_of_Scotland "Culture of Scotland"), [Wales](/wiki/Culture_of_Wales "Culture of Wales"), and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Culture_of_Northern_Ireland "Culture of Northern Ireland") are diverse and have varying degrees of overlap and distinctiveness.

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

Main article: [British cuisine](/wiki/British_cuisine "British cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Fish_and_chips.jpg/250px-Fish_and_chips.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fish_and_chips.jpg)

[Fish and chips](/wiki/Fish_and_chips "Fish and chips"), a popular [take-away food](/wiki/Take-away_food "Take-away food") throughout the United Kingdom, has been described as the quintessential British dish.[[216]](#cite_note-Walton-220)

Historically, [British cuisine](/wiki/British_cuisine "British cuisine") has meant "unfussy dishes made with quality local ingredients, matched with simple sauces to accentuate flavour, rather than disguise it".[[217]](#cite_note-221) It has been "vilified as unimaginative and heavy", and traditionally been limited in its international recognition to the [full breakfast](/wiki/Full_breakfast "Full breakfast") and the [Christmas dinner](/wiki/Christmas_dinner "Christmas dinner").[[218]](#cite_note-Spencer-222) This is despite British cuisine having absorbed the culinary influences of those who have [settled in Britain](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_Kingdom_since_1922 "Immigration to the United Kingdom since 1922"), resulting in hybrid dishes such as the [British Asian](/wiki/British_Asian "British Asian") [Chicken tikka masala](/wiki/Chicken_tikka_masala "Chicken tikka masala"), hailed by some as "Britain's true national dish".[[219]](#cite_note-bbcenc-223)

[Celtic agriculture](/wiki/Celtic_fields "Celtic fields") and animal breeding produced a wide variety of foodstuffs for Celts and Britons. The Anglo-Saxons developed meat and savoury herb stewing techniques before the practice became common in Europe. The [Norman conquest of England](/wiki/Norman_conquest_of_England "Norman conquest of England") introduced exotic spices into Britain in the Middle Ages.[[218]](#cite_note-Spencer-222) The British Empire facilitated a knowledge of [India's food tradition](/wiki/Indian_cuisine "Indian cuisine") of "strong, penetrating spices and herbs".[[218]](#cite_note-Spencer-222) [Food rationing policies](/wiki/Rationing_in_the_United_Kingdom "Rationing in the United Kingdom"), imposed by the British government during wartime periods of the 20th century, are said to have been the stimulus for British cuisine's poor international reputation.[[218]](#cite_note-Spencer-222)

British dishes include [fish and chips](/wiki/Fish_and_chips "Fish and chips"), the [Sunday roast](/wiki/Sunday_roast "Sunday roast"), and [bangers and mash](/wiki/Bangers_and_mash "Bangers and mash"). British cuisine has several national and regional varieties, including [English](/wiki/English_cuisine "English cuisine"), [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_cuisine "Scottish cuisine") and [Welsh cuisine](/wiki/Welsh_cuisine "Welsh cuisine"), each of which has developed its own regional or local dishes, many of which are [geographically indicated foods](/wiki/Geographically_indicated_foods_of_the_United_Kingdom "Geographically indicated foods of the United Kingdom") such as [Cheddar cheese](/wiki/Cheddar_cheese "Cheddar cheese"), [Cheshire cheese](/wiki/Cheshire_cheese "Cheshire cheese"), the [Yorkshire pudding](/wiki/Yorkshire_pudding "Yorkshire pudding"), [Arbroath Smokie](/wiki/Arbroath_Smokie "Arbroath Smokie"), [Cornish pasty](/wiki/Cornish_pasty "Cornish pasty") and [Welsh cakes](/wiki/Welsh_cake "Welsh cake").

The British are the second largest per capita [tea](/wiki/Tea "Tea") consumers in the world, consuming an average of 2.1 kilograms (4.6 lb) per person each year.[[220]](#cite_note-224) [British tea culture](/wiki/British_tea_culture "British tea culture") dates back to the 19th century, when India was part of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") and British interests controlled tea production in the subcontinent.

### Languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Languages")]

Further information: [British English](/wiki/British_English "British English"), [British literature](/wiki/British_literature "British literature"), and [Languages of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Languages_of_the_United_Kingdom "Languages of the United Kingdom")

There is no single British language, though [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") is by far the main language spoken by British citizens, being spoken monolingually by more than 70% of the UK population. English is therefore the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* official language of the United Kingdom.[[221]](#cite_note-225) However, under the [European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages](/wiki/European_Charter_for_Regional_or_Minority_Languages "European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages"), the [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"), [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic"), [Cornish](/wiki/Cornish_language "Cornish language"), [Irish Gaelic](/wiki/Irish_Gaelic "Irish Gaelic"), [Ulster Scots](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_dialects "Ulster Scots dialects"), [Manx](/wiki/Manx_language "Manx language") and [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language") languages are officially recognised as Regional or Minority languages by the UK Government.[[222]](#cite_note-226) [Insular varieties of Norman](/wiki/Norman_language "Norman language") are recognised languages of the Bailiwicks of Jersey and Guernsey, although they are dying.[[223]](#cite_note-227) Standard [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") is an official language of both bailiwicks.[[224]](#cite_note-228)[[225]](#cite_note-229)

As [indigenous languages](/wiki/Indigenous_language "Indigenous language") which continue to be spoken as a first language by native inhabitants, Welsh and Scottish Gaelic have a different legal status from other minority languages. In some parts of the UK, some of these languages are commonly spoken as a first language; in wider areas, their use in a bilingual context is sometimes supported or promoted by central or local government policy. For naturalisation purposes, a competence standard of English, Scottish Gaelic or Welsh is required to pass the [life in the United Kingdom test](/wiki/Life_in_the_United_Kingdom_test "Life in the United Kingdom test").[[226]](#cite_note-230) However, English is used routinely, and although considered culturally important, Scottish Gaelic and Welsh are much less used.

Throughout the United Kingdom there are distinctive spoken expressions and [regional accents of English](/wiki/Regional_accents_of_English "Regional accents of English"),[[41]](#cite_note-Rosen3-45) which are seen to be symptomatic of a locality's culture and identity.[[227]](#cite_note-231) An awareness and knowledge of accents in the United Kingdom can "place, within a few miles, the locality in which a man or woman has grown up".[[228]](#cite_note-Rose54-232)

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Literature")]

Main article: [British literature](/wiki/British_literature "British literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Jk-rowling-crop.JPG/250px-Jk-rowling-crop.JPG)](/wiki/File:Jk-rowling-crop.JPG)

[J.K. Rowling](/wiki/J.K._Rowling "J.K. Rowling") is one of the world's [best selling](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_fiction_authors "List of best-selling fiction authors") British authors. Her *[Harry Potter](/wiki/Harry_Potter "Harry Potter")* series of books have sold more than 400 million copies worldwide.[[229]](#cite_note-233)

[British literature](/wiki/British_literature "British literature") is "one of the leading literatures in the world".[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBroichBassnett200127-234) The overwhelming part is written in the [English language](/wiki/English_language "English language"), but there are also pieces of literature written in [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language"), [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic"), [Ulster Scots](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_dialects "Ulster Scots dialects"), [Cornish](/wiki/Cornish_literature "Cornish literature") and [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language").

Britain has a long history of famous and influential authors. It boasts some of the oldest pieces of literature in the Western world, such as the epic poem [Beowulf](/wiki/Beowulf "Beowulf"), one of the oldest surviving written work in the English language.[[231]](#cite_note-Cohen2008-235) Prior to the formation of British nationhood, famous authors who inhabited Great Britain include some of the world's most studied and praised writers. In England, the playwrights [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare") and [Christopher Marlowe](/wiki/Christopher_Marlowe "Christopher Marlowe") defined England's Elizabethan period.[[232]](#cite_note-Wagner2013-236)

The British Romantic movement was one of the strongest and most recognisable in Europe. The poets [William Blake](/wiki/William_Blake "William Blake"), [Robert Burns](/wiki/Robert_Burns "Robert Burns"), [Wordsworth](/wiki/William_Wordsworth "William Wordsworth") and [Coleridge](/wiki/Samuel_Taylor_Coleridge "Samuel Taylor Coleridge") were amongst the pioneers of Romanticism in literature.[[233]](#cite_note-Murray2013-237) Other Romantic writers that followed these figure further enhanced the profile of Romanticism in Europe, such as [John Keats](/wiki/John_Keats "John Keats"), [Percy Bysshe Shelley](/wiki/Percy_Bysshe_Shelley "Percy Bysshe Shelley") and [Lord Byron](/wiki/Lord_Byron "Lord Byron").[[234]](#cite_note-Snodgrass2009-238) Later periods like the Victorian Era saw a further flourishing of British writing, including [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens") and [William Thackeray](/wiki/William_Thackeray "William Thackeray").[[235]](#cite_note-Dahiya1992-239)

Women's literature in Britain has had a long and often troubled history, with many female writers producing work under a pen name, such as [George Eliot](/wiki/George_Eliot "George Eliot").[[236]](#cite_note-Levine2001-240) Other great female novelists that have contributed to world literature are [Frances Burney](/wiki/Frances_Burney "Frances Burney"), [Frances Hodgson Burnett](/wiki/Frances_Hodgson_Burnett "Frances Hodgson Burnett"), [Virginia Woolf](/wiki/Virginia_Woolf "Virginia Woolf"), [Jane Austen](/wiki/Jane_Austen "Jane Austen") and the Brontë sisters, [Emily](/wiki/Emily_Bront%C3%AB "Emily Brontë"), [Charlotte](/wiki/Charlotte_Bront%C3%AB "Charlotte Brontë") and [Anne](/wiki/Anne_Bront%C3%AB "Anne Brontë").[[237]](#cite_note-SheenKozlowski2004-241)

Non-fiction has also played an important role in the history of British letters, with the first dictionary of the English language being produced and compiled by [Samuel Johnson](/wiki/Samuel_Johnson "Samuel Johnson"), a graduate of Oxford University and a London resident.[[238]](#cite_note-Reddick1996-242)

### Media and music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Media and music")]

Further information: [Music of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Music_of_the_United_Kingdom "Music of the United Kingdom"), [Radio in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Radio_in_the_United_Kingdom "Radio in the United Kingdom"), and [Television in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Television_in_the_United_Kingdom "Television in the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Proms_in_the_Park_2.jpg/250px-Proms_in_the_Park_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Proms_in_the_Park_2.jpg)

[The Proms](/wiki/The_Proms "The Proms") is an eight-week summer season of daily orchestral classical music concerts held across the United Kingdom. The Last Night of the Proms celebrates British tradition with patriotic [classical music of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Classical_music_of_the_United_Kingdom "Classical music of the United Kingdom").[[239]](#cite_note-243)[[240]](#cite_note-244)

Although cinema, theatre, dance and live music are popular, the favourite pastime of the British is watching [television](/wiki/Television "Television").[[241]](#cite_note-245) Public broadcast [television in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Television_in_the_United_Kingdom "Television in the United Kingdom") began in 1936, with the launch of the BBC Television Service (now [BBC One](/wiki/BBC_One "BBC One")). In the United Kingdom and the [Crown dependencies](/wiki/Crown_dependencies "Crown dependencies"), one must have a [television licence](/wiki/Television_licensing_in_the_United_Kingdom "Television licensing in the United Kingdom") to legally receive any broadcast television service, from any source. This includes the commercial channels, cable and satellite transmissions, and the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet"). Revenue generated from the television licence is used to provide radio, television and Internet content for the [British Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/BBC "BBC"), and Welsh language television programmes for [S4C](/wiki/S4C "S4C"). The BBC, the common abbreviation of the British Broadcasting Corporation,[[242]](#cite_note-246) is the world's largest [broadcaster](/wiki/Broadcasting "Broadcasting").[[243]](#cite_note-247) Unlike other broadcasters in the UK, it is a [public service based](/wiki/Public_service_broadcasting_in_the_United_Kingdom "Public service broadcasting in the United Kingdom"), [quasi-autonomous](/wiki/Autonomy "Autonomy"), [statutory corporation](/wiki/Statutory_corporation "Statutory corporation") run by the [BBC Trust](/wiki/BBC_Trust "BBC Trust"). [Free-to-air](/wiki/Free-to-air "Free-to-air") terrestrial television channels available on a national basis are [BBC One](/wiki/BBC_One "BBC One"), [BBC Two](/wiki/BBC_Two "BBC Two"), [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(TV_channel) "ITV (TV channel)"), [Channel 4](/wiki/Channel_4 "Channel 4") ([S4C](/wiki/S4C "S4C") in Wales), and [Five](/wiki/Five_(TV_channel) "Five (TV channel)").

[100 Greatest British Television Programmes](/wiki/100_Greatest_British_Television_Programmes "100 Greatest British Television Programmes") was a list compiled by the [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute") in 2000, chosen by a poll of industry professionals, to determine what were the greatest British television programmes of any genre ever to have been screened.[[244]](#cite_note-greatestTV-248) Topping the list was *[Fawlty Towers](/wiki/Fawlty_Towers "Fawlty Towers")*, a [British sitcom](/wiki/British_sitcom "British sitcom") set in a fictional [Torquay](/wiki/Torquay "Torquay") hotel starring [John Cleese](/wiki/John_Cleese "John Cleese").[[244]](#cite_note-greatestTV-248)

"British musical tradition is essentially vocal",[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECosman195722-249) dominated by the [music of England](/wiki/English_folk_music "English folk music") and [Germanic culture](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages"),[[246]](#cite_note-250) most greatly influenced by [hymns](/wiki/Hymns "Hymns") and [Anglican church music](/wiki/Anglican_church_music "Anglican church music").[[247]](#cite_note-251) However, the specific, traditional [music of Wales](/wiki/Music_of_Wales "Music of Wales") and [music of Scotland](/wiki/Music_of_Scotland "Music of Scotland") is distinct, and of the [Celtic musical tradition](/wiki/Celtic_music "Celtic music").[[248]](#cite_note-252) In the United Kingdom, more people attend live music performances than football matches.[[249]](#cite_note-253) [British rock](/wiki/British_rock "British rock") was born in the mid-20th century out of the influence of [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll") and [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") from the United States. Major early exports were [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles"), [the Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones"), [the Who](/wiki/The_Who "The Who") and [the Kinks](/wiki/The_Kinks "The Kinks").[[250]](#cite_note-Else74-254) Together with other bands from the United Kingdom, these constituted the [British Invasion](/wiki/British_Invasion "British Invasion"), a popularisation of British pop and rock music in the United States. Into the 1970s [heavy metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music"), [new wave](/wiki/New_wave_music "New wave music"), and [2 tone](/wiki/2_tone_(type_of_music) "2 tone (type of music)").[[250]](#cite_note-Else74-254) [Britpop](/wiki/Britpop "Britpop") is a subgenre of [alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock") that emerged from the British [independent music](/wiki/Independent_music "Independent music") scene of the early 1990s and was characterised by bands reviving British guitar pop music of the 1960s and 1970s. Leading exponents of Britpop were [Blur](/wiki/Blur_(band) "Blur (band)"), [Oasis](/wiki/Oasis_(band) "Oasis (band)") and [Pulp](/wiki/Pulp_(band) "Pulp (band)").[[251]](#cite_note-Brit119-255) Also popularised in the United Kingdom during the 1990s were several domestically produced varieties of [electronic dance music](/wiki/Electronic_dance_music "Electronic dance music"); [acid house](/wiki/Acid_house "Acid house"), [UK hard house](/wiki/UK_hard_house "UK hard house"), [jungle](/wiki/Jungle_music "Jungle music"), [UK garage](/wiki/UK_garage "UK garage") which in turn have influenced [grime](/wiki/Grime_(music) "Grime (music)") and [British hip hop](/wiki/British_hip_hop "British hip hop") in the 2000s.[[251]](#cite_note-Brit119-255) [The BRIT Awards](/wiki/The_BRIT_Awards "The BRIT Awards") are the [British Phonographic Industry](/wiki/British_Phonographic_Industry "British Phonographic Industry")'s annual awards for both international and [British popular music](/wiki/British_popular_music "British popular music").

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Religion")]

Further information: [Religion in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Religion_in_the_United_Kingdom "Religion in the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Westminster_abbey_west.jpg/250px-Westminster_abbey_west.jpg)](/wiki/File:Westminster_abbey_west.jpg)

[Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey") is used for the [coronation of the British monarchs](/wiki/Coronation_of_the_British_monarch "Coronation of the British monarch"), who are also made the head of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England").

Historically, [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") has been the most influential and important religion in Britain, and it remains the declared faith of the majority of the British people.[[252]](#cite_note-Briton2001234-256) The influence of Christianity on British culture has been "widespread, extending beyond the spheres of prayer and worship. Churches and cathedrals make a significant contribution to the architectural landscape of the nation's cities and towns" whilst "many schools and hospitals were founded by men and women who were strongly influenced by Christian motives".[[252]](#cite_note-Briton2001234-256) Throughout the United Kingdom, [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter") and [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas"), the "two most important events in the Christian calendar", are recognised as [public holidays](/wiki/Holiday "Holiday").[[252]](#cite_note-Briton2001234-256)

Christianity remains the major religion of the population of the United Kingdom in the 21st century, followed by [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism") and then [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") in terms of numbers of adherents. The 2007 [Tearfund](/wiki/Tearfund "Tearfund") Survey revealed 53% identified themselves as Christian, which was similar to the 2004 [British Social Attitudes Survey](/wiki/British_Social_Attitudes_Survey "British Social Attitudes Survey"),[[253]](#cite_note-Tearfund_Survey-257)[[254]](#cite_note-BSAS2004-258) and to the [2001 United Kingdom census](/wiki/2001_United_Kingdom_census "2001 United Kingdom census") in which 71.6% said that Christianity was their religion,[[255]](#cite_note-259) However, the Tearfund Survey showed only one in ten Britons attend church weekly.[[256]](#cite_note-260) [Secularism](/wiki/Secularism "Secularism") was advanced in Britain during the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment"), and modern British organisations such as the [British Humanist Association](/wiki/British_Humanist_Association "British Humanist Association") and the [National Secular Society](/wiki/National_Secular_Society "National Secular Society") offer the opportunity for their members to "debate and explore the moral and philosophical issues in a non-religious setting".[[252]](#cite_note-Briton2001234-256)

The [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union") that led to the formation of the Kingdom of Great Britain ensured that there would be a [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") succession as well as a link between [church and state](/wiki/Separation_of_church_and_state "Separation of church and state") that still remains. The [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") ([Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican")) is legally recognised as the [established church](/wiki/Established_church "Established church"), and so retains representation in the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") through the [Lords Spiritual](/wiki/Lords_Spiritual "Lords Spiritual"), whilst the [British monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom") is a member of the church as well as its [Supreme Governor](/wiki/Supreme_Governor_of_the_Church_of_England "Supreme Governor of the Church of England").[[257]](#cite_note-Briton2001235-261)[[258]](#cite_note-262) The Church of England also retains the right to draft legislative measures (related to religious administration) through the [General Synod](/wiki/General_Synod_of_the_Church_of_England "General Synod of the Church of England") that can then be passed into law by Parliament. The [Roman Catholic Church in England and Wales](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church_in_England_and_Wales "Roman Catholic Church in England and Wales") is the second largest Christian church with around five million members, mainly in England.[[259]](#cite_note-263) There are also growing [Orthodox](/wiki/Orthodoxy "Orthodoxy"), [Evangelical](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism") and [Pentecostal](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism") churches, with Pentecostal churches in England now third after the Church of England and the Roman Catholic Church in terms of church attendance.[[260]](#cite_note-264) Other large Christian groups include [Methodists](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism") and [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists").

The [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism") [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland") (known informally as [The Kirk](/wiki/The_Kirk "The Kirk")), is recognised as the [national church](/wiki/National_church "National church") of Scotland and not subject to state control. The British monarch is an ordinary member and is required to swear an oath to "defend the security" of the church upon his or her accession. The [Roman Catholic Church in Scotland](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism_in_Scotland "Roman Catholicism in Scotland") is Scotland's second largest Christian church, with followers representing a sixth of the population of Scotland.[[261]](#cite_note-265) The [Scottish Episcopal Church](/wiki/Scottish_Episcopal_Church "Scottish Episcopal Church"), which is part of the Anglican Communion, dates from the final establishment of Presbyterianism in Scotland in 1690, when it split from the Church of Scotland over matters of theology and ritual. Further splits in the Church of Scotland, especially in the 19th century, led to the creation of other Presbyterian churches in Scotland, including the [Free Church of Scotland](/wiki/Free_Church_of_Scotland_(post-1900) "Free Church of Scotland (post-1900)"). In the 1920s, the [Church in Wales](/wiki/Church_in_Wales "Church in Wales") became independent from the Church of England and became '[disestablished'](/wiki/Disestablishmentarianism "Disestablishmentarianism") but remains in the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion").[[257]](#cite_note-Briton2001235-261) Methodism and other Protestant churches have had a major presence in Wales. The main [religious groups in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Religion_in_Northern_Ireland "Religion in Northern Ireland") are organised on an [all-Ireland](/wiki/All-Ireland "All-Ireland") basis. Though collectively Protestants constitute the overall majority,[[262]](#cite_note-266) the [Roman Catholic Church of Ireland](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism_in_Ireland "Roman Catholicism in Ireland") is the largest single church. The [Presbyterian Church in Ireland](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_Ireland "Presbyterian Church in Ireland"), closely linked to the Church of Scotland in terms of theology and history, is the second largest church followed by the [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland") (Anglican) which was disestablished in the 19th century.

### Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Sport")]

Further information: [Sport in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Sport_in_the_United_Kingdom "Sport in the United Kingdom")

See also: [Home Nations](/wiki/Home_Nations "Home Nations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/GB_Gold_WOC2008_Relay.jpg/250px-GB_Gold_WOC2008_Relay.jpg)](/wiki/File:GB_Gold_WOC2008_Relay.jpg)

The British gold medalist relay team of the 2008 [World Orienteering Championships](/wiki/World_Orienteering_Championships "World Orienteering Championships")

[Sport](/wiki/Sport "Sport") is an important element of British culture, and is one of the most popular leisure activities of Britons. Within the United Kingdom, nearly half of all adults partake in one or more sporting activity each week.[[263]](#cite_note-Briton2001282-267) Some of the major sports in the United Kingdom "were invented by the British",[[264]](#cite_note-Briton2001293-268) including [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union"), [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") and [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), and "exported various other games" including [tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis"), [badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton"), [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), [golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf"), [snooker](/wiki/Snooker "Snooker") and [squash](/wiki/Squash_(sport) "Squash (sport)").[[265]](#cite_note-Misc164-269)

In most sports, separate organisations, teams and clubs represent the individual [countries of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Countries of the United Kingdom") at international level, though in some sports, like rugby union, an all-Ireland team represents both Northern Ireland and Ireland (Republic of), and the [British and Irish Lions](/wiki/British_and_Irish_Lions "British and Irish Lions") represent Ireland and Britain as a whole. The UK is represented by a single team at the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") and at the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics"), the [Great Britain team](/wiki/Great_Britain_at_the_Olympics "Great Britain at the Olympics") won 65 medals: 29 gold (the most since the [1908 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1908_Summer_Olympics "1908 Summer Olympics")), 17 silver and 19 bronze, ranking them 3rd.[[266]](#cite_note-270) In total, sportsmen and women from the UK "hold over 50 world titles in a variety of sports, such as professional boxing, rowing, snooker, squash and motorcycle sports".[[263]](#cite_note-Briton2001282-267)

A 2006 poll found that association football was the most popular sport in the UK.[[267]](#cite_note-271) In England 320 football clubs are affiliated to [The Football Association](/wiki/The_Football_Association "The Football Association") (FA) and more than 42,000 clubs to regional or district associations. The FA, founded in 1863, and the Football League, founded in 1888, were both the first of their kind in the world.[[268]](#cite_note-Briton2001297-272) In Scotland there are 78 full and associate clubs and nearly 6,000 registered clubs under the jurisdiction of the [Scottish Football Association](/wiki/Scottish_Football_Association "Scottish Football Association").[[268]](#cite_note-Briton2001297-272) Two Welsh clubs play in England's Football League and others at non-league level, whilst the [Welsh Football League](/wiki/Welsh_Football_League "Welsh Football League") contains 20 semi-professional clubs. In Northern Ireland, 12 semi-professional clubs play in the [IFA Premiership](/wiki/IFA_Premiership "IFA Premiership"), the second oldest league in the world.[[268]](#cite_note-Briton2001297-272)

[Recreational fishing](/wiki/Recreational_fishing "Recreational fishing"), particularly [angling](/wiki/Angling "Angling"), is one of the most popular participation activities in the United Kingdom, with an estimated 3–4 million anglers in the country.[[264]](#cite_note-Briton2001293-268)[[269]](#cite_note-273) The most widely practised form of angling in [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales") is for [coarse fish](/wiki/Coarse_fishing "Coarse fishing") while in Scotland angling is usually for [salmon](/wiki/Salmon "Salmon") and [trout](/wiki/Trout "Trout").[[264]](#cite_note-Briton2001293-268)

### Visual art and architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Visual art and architecture")]

Further information: [Architecture of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Architecture_of_the_United_Kingdom "Architecture of the United Kingdom") and [Art of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Art_of_the_United_Kingdom "Art of the United Kingdom")

For centuries, artists and architects in Britain were overwhelmingly influenced by [Western art history](/wiki/Western_art_history "Western art history").[[270]](#cite_note-Else78-274) Amongst the first visual artists credited for developing a distinctly British aesthetic and artistic style is [William Hogarth](/wiki/William_Hogarth "William Hogarth").[[270]](#cite_note-Else78-274) The experience of military, political and economic power from the rise of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), led to a very specific drive in artistic technique, taste and sensibility in the United Kingdom.[[123]](#cite_note-taste-127) Britons used their art "to illustrate their knowledge and command of the natural world", whilst the permanent settlers in British North America, Australasia, and South Africa "embarked upon a search for distinctive artistic expression appropriate to their sense of national identity".[[123]](#cite_note-taste-127) The empire has been "at the centre, rather than in the margins, of the history of British art", and imperial British visual arts have been fundamental to the construction, celebration and expression of Britishness.[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarringer_et_al._20073-275)

British attitudes to [modern art](/wiki/Modern_art "Modern art") were "polarised" at the end of the 19th century.[[272]](#cite_note-Whittle5-276) Modernist movements were both cherished and vilified by artists and critics; [Impressionism](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism") was initially regarded by "many conservative critics" as a "subversive foreign influence", but became "fully assimilated" into British art during the early-20th century.[[272]](#cite_note-Whittle5-276) [Representational art](/wiki/Representation_(arts) "Representation (arts)") was described by [Herbert Read](/wiki/Herbert_Read "Herbert Read") during the [interwar period](/wiki/Interwar_period "Interwar period") as "necessarily... revolutionary", and was studied and produced to such an extent that by the 1950s, [Classicism](/wiki/Classicism "Classicism") was effectively void in British visual art.[[272]](#cite_note-Whittle5-276) [Post-modern](/wiki/Postmodern_art "Postmodern art"), contemporary British art, particularly that of the [Young British Artists](/wiki/Young_British_Artists "Young British Artists"), has been pre-occupied with [postcolonialism](/wiki/Postcolonialism "Postcolonialism"), and "characterised by a fundamental concern with material culture ... perceived as a post-imperial cultural anxiety".[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarringer_et_al._200717-277)

[Architecture of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Architecture_of_the_United_Kingdom "Architecture of the United Kingdom") is diverse; most influential developments have usually taken place in England, but Ireland, Scotland, and Wales have at various times played leading roles in architectural history.[[274]](#cite_note-BritEncart-278) Although there are prehistoric and classical structures in the British Isles, British architecture effectively begins with the first Anglo-Saxon Christian churches, built soon after [Augustine of Canterbury](/wiki/Augustine_of_Canterbury "Augustine of Canterbury") arrived in Great Britain in 597.[[274]](#cite_note-BritEncart-278) [Norman architecture](/wiki/Norman_architecture "Norman architecture") was built on a vast scale from the 11th century onwards in the form of castles and churches to help impose Norman authority upon their dominion.[[274]](#cite_note-BritEncart-278) [English Gothic architecture](/wiki/English_Gothic_architecture "English Gothic architecture"), which flourished from 1180 until c. 1520, was initially imported from France, but quickly developed its own unique qualities.[[274]](#cite_note-BritEncart-278) Secular [medieval architecture](/wiki/Medieval_architecture "Medieval architecture") throughout Britain has left a legacy of large stone [castles](/wiki/Castle "Castle"), with the "finest examples" being found lining both sides of the [Anglo-Scottish border](/wiki/Anglo-Scottish_border "Anglo-Scottish border"), dating from the [Wars of Scottish Independence](/wiki/Wars_of_Scottish_Independence "Wars of Scottish Independence") of the 14th century.[[275]](#cite_note-BritEncart2-279) The invention of gunpowder and canons made castles redundant, and the [English Renaissance](/wiki/English_Renaissance "English Renaissance") which followed facilitated the development of new artistic styles for domestic architecture: [Tudor style](/wiki/Tudor_style_architecture "Tudor style architecture"), [English Baroque](/wiki/English_Baroque "English Baroque"), [The Queen Anne Style](/wiki/Queen_Anne_Style_architecture "Queen Anne Style architecture") and [Palladian](/wiki/Palladian_architecture "Palladian architecture").[[275]](#cite_note-BritEncart2-279) [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_architecture "Georgian architecture") and [Neoclassical architecture](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") advanced after the [Scottish Enlightenment](/wiki/Scottish_Enlightenment "Scottish Enlightenment"). Outside the United Kingdom, the influence of British architecture is particularly strong in [South India](/wiki/South_India "South India"),[[276]](#cite_note-Sing69-280) the result of [British rule in India](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") in the 19th century. The Indian cities of [Bangalore](/wiki/Bangalore "Bangalore"), [Chennai](/wiki/Chennai "Chennai"), and [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai") each have courts, hotels and [train stations](/wiki/Train_station "Train station") designed in British architectural styles of [Gothic Revivalism](/wiki/Gothic_Revival_architecture "Gothic Revival architecture") and [neoclassicism](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture").[[276]](#cite_note-Sing69-280)

### Political culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Political culture")]

Further information: [Politics of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Politics_of_the_United_Kingdom "Politics of the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Palace_of_Westminster%2C_London_-_Feb_2007.jpg/250px-Palace_of_Westminster%2C_London_-_Feb_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palace_of_Westminster,_London_-_Feb_2007.jpg)

The [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster") is a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") which houses the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom").

British political culture is tied closely with its institutions and [civics](/wiki/Civics "Civics"), and a "subtle fusion of new and old values".[[209]](#cite_note-Craith169-213)[[277]](#cite_note-281) The principle of [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"), with its notions of stable [parliamentary government](/wiki/Parliamentary_government "Parliamentary government") and [political liberalism](/wiki/Political_liberalism "Political liberalism"), "have come to dominate British culture".[[278]](#cite_note-282) These views have been reinforced by Sir [Bernard Crick](/wiki/Bernard_Crick "Bernard Crick") who said:[[139]](#cite_note-Brad34-143)

> To be British seems to us to mean that we respect the laws, the elected parliamentary and democratic political structures, traditional values of mutual tolerance, respect for equal rights and mutual concern; that we give our allegiance to the state (as commonly symbolised by [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown")) in return for its protection.

British political institutions include the [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system"), the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") and [Privy Council of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Privy_Council_of_the_United_Kingdom "Privy Council of the United Kingdom").[[279]](#cite_note-283) Although the Privy Council is primarily a British institution, officials from other Commonwealth realms are also appointed to the body.[[280]](#cite_note-gay-p3-284) The most notable continuing instance is the [Prime Minister of New Zealand](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_New_Zealand "Prime Minister of New Zealand"), its senior politicians, Chief Justice and Court of Appeal judges are conventionally made Privy Counsellors,[[281]](#cite_note-nz-pc-285) as the prime ministers and chief justices of Canada and Australia used to be.[[282]](#cite_note-286)[[283]](#cite_note-287) Prime Ministers of Commonwealth countries which retain the [British monarch](/wiki/British_monarch "British monarch") as their sovereign continue to be sworn as Privy Counsellors.[[280]](#cite_note-gay-p3-284)

[Universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage") for all males over 21 was granted in 1918 and for adult women in 1928 after the [Suffragette movement](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage_in_the_United_Kingdom "Women's suffrage in the United Kingdom").[[284]](#cite_note-288) Politics in the United Kingdom is [multi-party](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system"), with three dominant political parties: the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)"), the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") and the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)"). The [social structure of Britain](/wiki/Social_structure_of_Britain "Social structure of Britain"), specifically [social class](/wiki/Social_class "Social class"), has "long been pre-eminent among the factors used to explain party allegiance", and still persists as "the dominant basis" of party political allegiance for Britons.[[285]](#cite_note-289) The Conservative Party is descended from the historic [Tory Party](/wiki/Tory_Party "Tory Party") (founded in England in 1678), and is a [centre-right](/wiki/Centre-right "Centre-right") [conservative](/wiki/Conservative "Conservative") political party,[[286]](#cite_note-290) which traditionally draws support from the [middle classes](/wiki/Middle_class "Middle class").[[287]](#cite_note-291) The Labour Party (founded by Scotsman [Keir Hardie](/wiki/Keir_Hardie "Keir Hardie")) grew out of the [trade union](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union") movement and [socialist](/wiki/Socialist "Socialist") political parties of the 19th century, and continues to describe itself as a "democratic socialist party".[[288]](#cite_note-iqgxtf-292) Labour states that it stands for the representation of the low-paid [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class"), who have traditionally been its members and voters.[[288]](#cite_note-iqgxtf-292) The [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)") are a [liberal](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism") political party, and third largest in the UK in terms of MPs elected. It is descended from the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)"), a major [ruling party](/wiki/Ruling_party "Ruling party") of 19th-century UK through to the First World War, when it was supplanted by the Labour Party.[[289]](#cite_note-Dear102-293) The Liberal Democrats have historically drawn support from wide and "differing social backgrounds".[[289]](#cite_note-Dear102-293) The [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") is the fourth largest political party in the UK in terms of representation in parliament, having won 9 out of 57 Scottish seats at the 2024 General Election.[[290]](#cite_note-294) There are over 300 other, smaller [political parties in the United Kingdom](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of political parties in the United Kingdom") registered to the [Electoral Commission](/wiki/Electoral_Commission_(United_Kingdom) "Electoral Commission (United Kingdom)").[[291]](#cite_note-295)[[292]](#cite_note-296)

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_people&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Classification")]

See also: [British nationality law](/wiki/British_nationality_law "British nationality law"), [Ethnic groups in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_the_United_Kingdom "Ethnic groups in the United Kingdom"), and [United Kingdom Census 2001 Ethnic Codes](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Census_2001_Ethnic_Codes "United Kingdom Census 2001 Ethnic Codes")

According to the [British Social Attitudes Survey](/wiki/British_Social_Attitudes_Survey "British Social Attitudes Survey"), there are broadly two interpretations of British identity, with ethnic and civic dimensions:

> The first group, which we term the ethnic dimension, contained the items about birthplace, ancestry, living in Britain, and sharing British customs and traditions. The second, or civic group, contained the items about feeling British, respecting laws and institutions, speaking English, and having British citizenship.[[293]](#cite_note-297)

Of the two perspectives of British identity, the civic definition has become "the dominant idea ... by far",[[113]](#cite_note-Ichijo26-117) and in this capacity, Britishness is sometimes considered an institutional or overarching [state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state") identity.[[112]](#cite_note-Langlands-116)[[113]](#cite_note-Ichijo26-117)[[139]](#cite_note-Brad34-143) This has been used to explain why first-, second- and third-generation immigrants are more likely to describe themselves as British, rather than English, because it is an "institutional, inclusive" identity, that can be acquired through [naturalisation](/wiki/Naturalisation "Naturalisation") and [British nationality law](/wiki/British_nationality_law "British nationality law");[[294]](#cite_note-Ichijo27-298) the vast majority of people in the United Kingdom who are from an ethnic minority feel British.[[295]](#cite_note-299)

However, this attitude is more common in England than in Scotland or Wales; "white English people perceived themselves as English first and as British second, and most people from ethnic minority backgrounds perceived themselves as British, but none identified as English, a label they associated exclusively with white people". Contrawise, in Scotland and Wales, [White British](/wiki/White_British "White British") and ethnic minority people both identified more strongly with Scotland and Wales than with Britain.[[296]](#cite_note-300)

Studies and surveys have "reported that the majority of the Scots and Welsh see themselves as both Scottish/Welsh and British though with some differences in emphasis".[[294]](#cite_note-Ichijo27-298) The [Commission for Racial Equality](/wiki/Commission_for_Racial_Equality "Commission for Racial Equality") found that with respect to notions of [nationality](/wiki/Nationality "Nationality") in Britain, "the most basic, objective and uncontroversial conception of the British people is one that includes the English, the Scots and the Welsh".[[297]](#cite_note-CRE22-301) However, "English participants tended to think of themselves as indistinguishably English or British, while both Scottish and Welsh participants identified themselves much more readily as Scottish or Welsh than as British".[[297]](#cite_note-CRE22-301)

Some persons opted "to combine both identities" as "they felt Scottish or Welsh, but held a [British passport](/wiki/British_passport "British passport") and were therefore British", whereas others saw themselves as exclusively Scottish or exclusively Welsh and "felt quite divorced from the British, whom they saw as the English".[[297]](#cite_note-CRE22-301) Commentators have described this latter phenomenon as "[nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism")", a rejection of British identity because some Scots and Welsh interpret it as "cultural imperialism imposed" upon the United Kingdom by "English ruling elites",[[298]](#cite_note-Ward2-3-302) or else a response to a historical misappropriation of equating the word "English" with "British",[[299]](#cite_note-303) which has "brought about a desire among Scots, Welsh and Irish to learn more about their heritage and distinguish themselves from the broader British identity".[[300]](#cite_note-304)
